title: #architecture: 7088881234
preview:
Nora Blake: Quick sync — I want to propose a compact contract for route lifecycle that reduces ambiguity between policy authors and the runtime. Too many incidents from routes disappearing.
Ethan Park: +1. High level: policy expresses intent; runtime enforces safety. We need explicit fields and a clear clamp/audit mechanism.
Maya Torres: Suggested minimal data model: `intent_expiry_seconds` (nullable), `soft_deprecate_seconds`, `prune_after_seconds`. Runtime exposes `safety_cap_seconds` per deployment and may shorten any intent but must log `consensus_clamp` with `actor` and `reason`.
Sven Müller: For private/on-prem installs add signed intent bundles. Control plane signs; local admin can sign for air-gapped installs. Runtime validates signature and rejects expired bundles.
Nora Blake: Coordinator question: who schedules prunes? Control plane preferred for hosted customers; local runtime for local-only customers. We should support both but keep canonical precedence: control-plane > local.
Ethan Park: Implementation detail: when control-plane is present it publishes a `prune-plan` in batches. Runtimes can `dry-run` the plan and report back before enforcement. Dry-run mode also helps private customers validate.
Maya Torres: Telemetry contract: events `route.intent_declared`, `route.intent_clamped`, `route.soft_deprecated`, `route.pruned`. Fields: policy_id, route_id, declared_ttl, effective_ttl, clamp_reason, runtime_version, deployment_mode. Keep payload small.
Sven Müller: API ergonomics: add `GET /v1/policies/{id}/prune-preview?window=72h` and CLI `redwood route preview --policy-id`. Also runtime should surface header `X-Redwood-Route-Deprecation: soft;eta=2026-03-12T12:00:00Z` during soft-deprecate window.
Nora Blake: Linting rules: reject `ttl: 0` unless `permanent: true`. Warn when legacy `ttl` used; require new policies to include `intent_*` fields. Add auto-fix suggestion in CI.
Ethan Park: Emergency ops: `operator_override` endpoint that is timeboxed (auto-expire 1h) and requires an attached post-mortem ticket. Overrides must appear in telemetry as `operator_override:<id>`.
Maya Torres: Migration plan: 1) accept both legacy `ttl` and new `intent_*` fields and emit warnings; 2) enable linter enforcement for PRs; 3) after grace period start enforcing clamps only on new routes, then migrate existing routes via console-assisted flow.
Sven Müller: Testing: add `route-lifecycle-sim` harness to exercise active->soft->prune transitions, signed-bundle verification tests, and clamp audit propagation. Include integration test that validates header emission and audit events.
Nora Blake: Rollout: feature-flag `route-intent-lifecycle-v2`. Start internal, then opt-in beta, then default. Provide a clear opt-out for enterprise/private customers.
Ethan Park: Docs checklist: data model (examples), CLI commands, migration steps, operator runbook for overrides, and signed-bundle key rotation guide for private customers.
Maya Torres: Action items: I'll draft the schema + linter PR. Sven prototype telemetry and signed-bundle flow. Ethan draft preview API + CLI. OpsBot: create epic for rollout.
OpsBot: Epic created RW-PLT-9001 "Intent-first route lifecycle & prune plan". Assigned owners and initial milestones.
Nora Blake: Revisit in 4 working days after PRs land. Thanks everyone. :thumbsup:

title: #architecture: 4891234567
preview:
Samir Rao: FYI a customer hit repeated defer loops when the routing policy kept sending retries to the low-cost pool. Saw client timeouts. Need a quick design decision on preventing re-defer loops. :frowning:

Priya Natarajan: Can you share the trace? My initial thought: we need a per-decision marker so retries know a defer already happened.

Samir Rao: Trace: ```[2026-03-20T13:10:02Z] decision id=9f3de defer_to=pool-low-cost
[2026-03-20T13:10:06Z] client_timeout id=9f3de
[2026-03-20T13:10:06Z] retry parent=abc123 -> decision id=8aa21 defer_to=pool-low-cost
```

Maya Lin: Two policy options: (A) Central policy adds a "defer_window_ms" and runtime honors it; (B) runtime tracks ephemeral token and forbids re-defer for same parent_request_id.

Priya Natarajan: I prefer runtime enforcement with central defaults. Faster, fewer RPCs. But we need token signing for private installs and a telemetry flag to validate.

Samir Rao: Agreed. Also add a fallback strategy enum: [immediate-fallback, hedge-first, retry-later]. Default could be immediate-fallback when parent ID seen.

Maya Lin: Telemetry fields to add: decision_id, parent_request_id, defer_window_ms, defer_hit (bool), token_sig_ok (bool), latency_buckets=[0-5,5-20,20-100,100-500,500+].

Priya Natarajan: Implementation plan: runtime adds small signed token {decision_id, exp_ms}; central controller ships default defer_window and max_window. Canary 5% traffic, monitor client timeout rate and tenant cost delta.

infra-bot: Deploy queued: sidecar/0.12.3 -> canary(5%) on 2026-03-22T01:00:00Z. Release notes: defer token + telemetry + defer_window support.

Samir Rao: Action items: @Priya build token HMAC library, @Maya update policy schema + telemetry docs, I'll prepare canary dashboards and incident replay.

Priya Natarajan: Will PR HMAC lib later today.

Maya Lin: Updating docs and CI tests now.

Samir Rao: Thanks — regroup after 48h metrics.

title: #architecture: 4623512345
preview:
Alice Nguyen: Heads up — thinking about consolidating the current "mux" routing adapters into a single multiplexing abstraction. Idea: unify shard-aware, cost-aware, and geo-routing under one minimal API so we can add new heuristics without adapter explosion.

Ben Carter: +1 on consolidation. Can you sketch what the abstraction looks like? worried about performance and lock contention.

Alice Nguyen: rough sketch:
```
mux.Resolve(request) -> [RouteCandidate{id, score, metadata}]
mux.CacheHint(key) -> prefetch/kvcache keys
```
Edge vs coordinator separation: edge does quick heuristic; coordinator runs heavier scoring.

Carla M: @Alice what's the fallback story? we still need per-tenant overrides and canary policy.

Alice Nguyen: Plan:
- minimal runtime API (Resolve/Score/Explain)
- plugin layer for tenant overrides (sandboxed)
- compat-shim for old adapters
- rollout via header: X-Routing-Mode: mux_v1 or legacy

Dmitri Ivanov: How do we avoid double-counting cost/latency metrics? telemetry needs a "decision_event" with inputs and final score. also keep sampling low overhead.

Eve Li: Agree. Also consider moving heavy scoring to async background re-ranker so latency SLOs maintained.

Ben Carter: Implementation concerns:
- batching of Resolve calls to reduce DB/redis lookups
- kernel: in-memory candidate pool per region
- prefer no global locks; use compare-and-swap for lease updates

infra-bot: perf CI for mux branch: all tests passed (mux/*) — run 45m, p95 latency increase 0.8% :eyes:

Carla M: About plugin layer — sandboxing options?
- WASM plugin (safe, fast)
- Lua in-process (fast but riskier)
- remote policy service (language-agnostic but network bound)

Alice Nguyen: leaning WASM for safety. Will start prototype: rust -> wasm. Need eval harness.

Ben Carter: For compatibility, let's keep adapter facade:
```
if header == "legacy": adapter.LegacyResolve(req)
else: mux.Resolve(req)
```
and add feature flag to flip per-tenant.

Dmitri Ivanov: Two risky things: 1) telemetry explosion (we'll need to limit explain traces), 2) rollout speed: we must be able to kill mux path quickly.

Eve Li: Propose kill-switch via control-plane flag + fallback to legacy with zero-impact on req path.

Carla M: Also require perf gating: mux rollout only if qps bench < 5% regress, mem delta < 100MB/pod.

Alice Nguyen: Work items (first cut):
1) proto for Resolve/Score/Explain (PR)
2) WASM plugin harness + sample tenant policy
3) compat-shim + feature flag path
4) perf harness + CI gating
5) telemetry schema + sample size cap

Ben Carter: timeline? Can we get an initial prototype in 3 weeks? :timer_clock:

Alice Nguyen: 3 weeks for proto + WASM POC sounds doable. Will add ticket in RFC board. I'll @Ben and @Carla for pre-review.

Carla M: :thumbsup: Add a test that simulates regional failover + constrained capacity.

Dmitri Ivanov: We'll need to update console to show mux decisions (explain) — add to roadmap.

Alice Nguyen: noted. I'll post a short followup RFC doc with config examples and schemas.

infra-bot: deploy-bot: canary mux-v1 rolled to 10% tenants at 2026-03-21T15:22Z

title: #architecture: 2123456789
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick follow-up from last week's multi-region sync — I'm proposing we add a lightweight 'x-region-stamp' header plus a control-plane residency policy endpoint so edge proxies can do fast local-first routing without holding heavy locks or leases. Thoughts?

Matt Johnson: curious — how is this different from our lease swap flow? seems like duplication.

Ava Chen: Good call. The lease swap is still necessary for shard ownership. This stamp is for per-request routing hints for stateless proxies and CDN-like edges where we want best-effort locality and fast fallback. TTL-based, cryptographically signed, no long-lived ownership. :thinking:

Sophia Patel: Love the idea. Example header sketch:
```
x-region-stamp: region=us-west-2; tenant=acme; exp=1712345600; sig=MEFa...
```
Proxies validate sig and expiry, then prefer local pool if policy allows.

Kai Nguyen: How do we prevent someone spoofing the header and bypassing residency controls?

Ava Chen: Stamp is issued by control-plane KMS per-org and signed with an HMAC/ECDSA key. Edge runtime caches public keys and rejects stamps not verifiable or expired. We'll also reject stamps for tenants flagged as "legal_hold" in policy.

Matt Johnson: When policy says "local-only" (regulatory), do we still route to other regions when local pool is saturated?

Sophia Patel: No. Policy table will have enforcement levels: local-only, local-preferred, any. local-only => route only to resident region; if capacity exhausted, return 429/error or queue depending on tenant SLA. local-preferred => attempt local, fallback to non-resident if stamp expired or explicitly allowed.

Tanvi Rao: Rollout plan idea: emit stamps from control-plane on auth/token exchange and on dedicated handshake. Stamps short-lived (~30s-2m) so failover happens quickly without manual lease revokes. Also add a flag in policy: preserve-residency-for-legal-hold = true to force local-only even during emergency.

Sophia Patel: Minor implementation note: routers will need a small verification cache and a fast path when header absent (treat as default policy lookup by tenant). We'll add a token parser that returns {region, tenant, exp, sig} and a boolean verified.

Ava Chen: Security/threats: Kai -> can you draft a short threat model focusing on stamp replay, key compromise, and cross-tenant spoofing?

Kai Nguyen: On it. Quick suggestions: include per-tenant key rotation, nonce or per-issue counter to detect reuse in high-value flows, and KMS audit hooks.

Matt Johnson: Migration concerns: how do we transition existing proxies? we can't flip all at once.

Ava Chen: Plan: 1) implement stamp-verify as opt-in behind config flag; 2) deploy parser and metrics; 3) enable for a small cohort of tenants with local-preferred; 4) enable enforcement mode for local-only tenants only after we validate metrics. I'll write RFC with rollout steps.

Sophia Patel: Also propose an API on control-plane: /residency/policy?tenant=acme returns one-line policy + signing key id. Keep it tiny to avoid coupling.

Tanvi Rao: For analytics, emit tags: stamp_verified, stamp_region, stamp_policy_level. That helps us measure cost/regional hit-rate. :chart_with_upwards_trend:

router-bot: deploy queued for branch feat/region-stamp-parser on staging (build 4837) :rocket:

Ava Chen: Action items: Ava -> draft RFC + sample header spec; Sophia -> prototype parser + metrics; Kai -> threat model; Matt -> migration checklist + SLA implications; Tanvi -> observability triggers. I'll schedule a short sync next Tues. Thanks all. :thumbsup:

title: #architecture: 1829998888
preview:
alex chen: quick follow-up from the RFC — i pushed the "cohesion-by-contract" proposal to the doc (link: https://docs.internal/rfcs/cohesion-by-contract). tl;dr: define minimal runtime contracts between hotpath modules and enforce with adapters rather than big-service splits.

priya natarajan: saw it, thanks. can you summarize the big tradeoffs? i'm worried about operational complexity if we add an adapter per module.

alex chen: sure — tradeoff bullets: 1) adapters add a small surface but let us keep services cohesive; 2) faster incremental rollouts vs big-cut rewrites; 3) slight latency cost for adapter hop but much lower overall regression risk.

michael ford: do we have perf numbers for the adapter hop? even 1-2ms on the hotpath adds up.

alex chen: good question. rough bench from spike: adapter baseline ~0.6ms median on p3-like nodes, tail depends on serialization. i put the benchmark tables in the doc. we can do a lighter binary protocol to shave another ~0.2ms.

jordan lee: how are we enforcing contracts? are we adding compile-time checks or runtime validation? both?

alex chen: proposal is a hybrid: compile-time schemas + runtime asserts in dev/staging. production will have a lightweight validation layer that fails fast and streams violation metrics to console.

samir patel: for tech-debt-paydown, can we schedule the adapter migrations as small tickets that each team owns? i'd rather see 2-week windows to remove old code per module.

priya natarajan: agreed on ownership. also we should add a migration checklist: feature-flag, perf-run, canary, kill-legacy. can you add that to the doc, alex?

alex chen: added checklist to the doc under "migration playbook". short version: 1) add adapter + schema 2) run perf + lint 3) feature flag on 10% 4) 72h monitoring 5) ramp 6) remove legacy.

michael ford: what about observability? adapters need consistent tracing and token-level cost visibility.

alex chen: yep — adapters will propagate span context and attach a small metadata tag "contract-v". logs will be tagged, and console will show per-contract latencies.

jordan lee: this helps with decoupling. one more risk: test surface explosion. if every adapter has its own tests, our CI time could blow up.

samir patel: suggestion: adopt contract-level unit tests + a single integration harness that exercises many adapters in parallel. also use selective CI (only run heavy tests on adapter changes).

priya natarajan: sounds reasonable. can we get a phased timeline? i.e., which modules first?

alex chen: suggested phase 0: session-router, kv-cache gateway and request-router adapters (low surface, high coupling wins). phase 1: enrichment adapters, then longer-tail services. timeline in doc approximated at 6 months for core hotpath paydown.

michael ford: :+1: for starting with session-router. will pair on perf tuning.

deploy-bot: Document updated by alex chen (cohesion-by-contract). View at https://docs.internal/rfcs/cohesion-by-contract (v3).

samir patel: i'll open tickets and assign owners next week. want to sync w/ infra on canary windows.

priya natarajan: cool — let's have a 30min sync Wed to lock the phase 0 owners and blockers. please add agenda items to the doc.

alex chen: done. thanks all — i'll post the sync invite and attach short perf slides.

michael ford: thanks, alex. excited to see debt get paid down without a risky big-bang.

samir patel: suggestion: adopt contract-level unit tests + a single integration harness that exercises many adapters in parallel. also use selective CI (only run heavy tests on adapter changes).

priya natarajan: sounds reasonable. can we get a phased timeline? i.e., which modules first?

alex chen: suggested phase 0: session-router, kv-cache gateway and request-router adapters (low surface, high coupling wins). phase 1: enrichment adapters, then longer-tail services. timeline in doc approximated at 6 months for core hotpath paydown.

michael ford: :+1: for starting with session-router. will pair on perf tuning.

deploy-bot: Document updated by alex chen (cohesion-by-contract). View at https://docs.internal/rfcs/cohesion-by-contract (v3).

samir patel: i'll open tickets and assign owners next week. want to sync w/ infra on canary windows.

priya natarajan: cool — let's have a 30min sync Wed to lock the phase 0 owners and blockers. please add agenda items to the doc.

alex chen: done. thanks all — i'll post the sync invite and attach short perf slides.

michael ford: thanks, alex. excited to see debt get paid down without a risky big-bang.

title: #architecture: 1776417129
preview:
Claire Dubois: Dropping ADR-019 for review: FP8/INT8 kernel auto-selection heuristics + rollout guardrails. Would like architecture sign-off today so Yuki can land the dispatch changes. Link: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ARCH/ADR-019-FP8-INT8-kernel-selection-heuristics

Claire Dubois: Key asks:
- Are the selection predicates defensible (GPU arch + shape regime + seq-len split)?
- Are fallbacks/guardrails sufficient to avoid customer-impacting regressions?
- Anything missing re: reproducibility / canary thresholds?

Noah Patel: Read through. Overall direction good, but I want to sanity check two things:
1) “prefer FP8 on H100 for decode-heavy if head_dim in {128, 64}” — we need a hard guard for KV cache alignment + stride; otherwise we’ll rediscover the edge case from earlier.
2) We should be explicit on how we detect “decode-heavy”: is it request-level (observed tokens) or workload classification in scheduler? If it’s inferred, it’ll be noisy.

Hiro Tanaka: +1 on KV alignment. Grace has a patch in flight for the alignment edge case but we should still guard.
Re decode-heavy: in the ADR we wrote it as “seq_len regime (prefill vs decode) known at kernel dispatch boundary” because we dispatch separately for prefill and decode paths.

Grace Kim: Confirmed: dispatch is called with phase=Prefill/Decode already. No need to infer from traffic.
KV alignment guard: we can add a quick check (kv_cache_bytes % 256 == 0) + shape constraints. If not satisfied -> fallback to baseline kernels.

Yuki Sato: On heuristics: current draft for H100:
- Attention: FP8 for decode when head_dim=128 and seq_len<=4k; baseline for long-context decode; for prefill, FP8 only if batch>=2 and prompt_len distribution is “short/med”.
- Matmul: INT8 for M in {1,2,4} decode GEMMs w/ alignment; FP8 for larger M prefill GEMMs.
Does that match architecture expectations or too complicated?

Sofia Ivanova: From scheduling POV: careful with “batch>=2” as a gate for FP8 prefill. With continuous batching, the instantaneous microbatch size might be 1 even if the system is loaded.
If we gate by observed microbatch we’ll flap. Better to gate by scheduler mode/config (e.g., mixed workload, max_batch_tokens, batching window) rather than instantaneous batch.

Julian Moore: Agree. We can expose a “batching_enabled” / “expected_batching” bit in the dispatch context (derived from scheduler config). We added the prefill/decode split metrics in PR-19855; can add a low-cardinality tag for “scheduler_policy=mixed|prefill-priority|decode-priority”.

Noah Patel: Complexity is fine if it’s testable and stable. But please don’t key dispatch off counters that are inherently noisy.
Also: we need “safe default” behavior when dims are unknown / unsupported (e.g., odd hidden sizes from finetunes). ADR says fallback, but we should call out: fallback is bf16 kernels with known correctness.

Rina Kobayashi: On testability: can we commit to a small “shape table” unit test set?
Example: (hidden=8192, heads=64, head_dim=128, seq=2048) and (hidden=12288, head_dim=128, seq=8192) across H100/A100.
We’ve had regressions where a predicate change accidentally caught long-context cases.

Hiro Tanaka: Yep. Yuki already has unit tests in PR-19863 for representative shapes; we can add the long-context decode case explicitly as “must NOT pick FP8”.

Jonas Weber: Applied ML request: ADR should include “quality gates” for default enablement (not just perf).
We’ve seen model-specific FP8 long-context issues. Even if perf is great, we need an eval sign-off for enabling by default per model family.
If this is only “auto-select behind flag” then fine, but we should be crystal clear: the heuristic is *eligible* selection, not *default enablement policy*.

Claire Dubois: Good call. I’ll add a note: selection heuristics define what’s technically safe/perf-optimal; product defaults still require eval sign-off per policy doc.

Allison Grant: Canary/observability: ADR mentions TTFT/TPOT but doesn’t state thresholds. Even high-level would help:
- rollback if p95 TTFT +10% for 15m in canary cohort
- rollback if error rate +0.2% absolute
And note: we need dimension tags (model, gpu_sku, kernel_mode) or canary is blind.

Rafael Mendes: +1. From SRE standpoint, the guardrails should require:
- enablement by model+region (not global)
- automatic rollback on sustained breach
- manual override documented
Also: for “fallback rules” I want to see the exact ordering:
FP8 -> INT8 -> baseline, and what conditions cause each fallback (unsupported shape, correctness check fail, perf canary event, etc.)

Yuki Sato: For ordering: current plan is per-op.
- Attention decode: prefer FP8 where supported; else baseline (we’re not planning INT8 attention this sprint).
- Matmul decode: prefer INT8 where supported; else baseline.
We can implement a “global kill switch” for fp8_attention and int8_matmul separately.

Noah Patel: Please avoid cross-op coupling (e.g., “if attention is FP8 then matmul must be FP8”). Keep it per-op to reduce blast radius.

Sofia Ivanova: Another rollout nuance: scheduler knobs may be required to realize perf wins. If we ship kernel changes + scheduler changes at the same time, canary attribution gets messy.
ADR suggests shipping behind flags; can we stage:
1) land kernel flags (off)
2) canary kernels alone
3) then canary scheduler tweaks

Hiro Tanaka: That’s the plan. Kernel path flags first; scheduler knob defaults in a separate flag group. We can do separate cohorts.

Grace Kim: Alternative heuristic idea: rather than hard-coded shape thresholds, we could consult microbench lookup tables keyed by (gpu, op, shape bucket) and choose the fastest.
But I think too heavy for runtime hot path right now.

Noah Patel: Agree with not doing lookup-table dispatch in this sprint. But ADR should mention “future work: data-driven dispatch” and ensure current rule-based is conservative.

Rina Kobayashi: Another concern: driver/CUDA differences. If H100 cluster A is on a slightly different driver, FP8 kernel perf can flip.
Can we add in ADR: benchmarks must pin container+driver for claims; runtime heuristics shouldn’t depend on perf deltas observed in one environment.

Claire Dubois: Yes. Adding a section: reproducibility + non-goals (heuristics are based on architecture + known kernel constraints, not fragile microbench wins).

Allison Grant: For canary: please ensure we tag metrics with “kernel_mode=fp8|int8|baseline” AND “kernel_id” if possible (even a short enum). Otherwise debugging is painful.

Julian Moore: Kernel_id tag might be high-cardinality if it includes shape. But we can tag “kernel_impl=flashattn_fp8_v2” / “gemm_int8_cutlass_x” etc. Controlled enum.

Rafael Mendes: Works. And add a runbook step: how to disable per region/model quickly.

Jonas Weber: One more: correctness checks. ADR mentions “correctness fallback”, but what does that mean at runtime?
We can’t do per-request golden compare. So it should mean “offline correctness suite + CI smoke” and runtime fallback only for explicit unsupported shapes / internal kernel error codes.

Hiro Tanaka: Exactly. Runtime fallback is based on capability checks + kernel return status; correctness is enforced in CI + perf canary catches systemic regressions.

Noah Patel: Then the ADR should say that explicitly so it’s not misread as “runtime validates correctness.”

Yuki Sato: Action items I’ll take from this thread:
- Add explicit KV cache alignment/stride guards (attention decode)
- Remove “instantaneous batch>=2” gating; switch to scheduler policy bit
- Add long-context “do not FP8” test case
- Clarify fallback order and kill switches per op
- Add section: correctness enforcement (CI/evals) vs runtime capability fallback

Claire Dubois: Great. Once those edits are in, I’m comfortable marking ADR-019 approved.

Rafael Mendes: Same, with the caveat that canary + rollback language is included (even if thresholds live in the runbook). :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: +1. I’ll sync with Julian on metric tags and update dashboard spec.

Jonas Weber: Approved pending the “eligible vs default enablement” clarification + link to eval sign-off criteria.

Hiro Tanaka: Thanks all. We’ll incorporate and post updated ADR link here for final ack.

Claire Dubois: :white_check_mark: Please tag me on the update; I’ll add the decision note + date at the end of the ADR once merged.

title: #architecture: 1770219804
preview:
Noah Patel: Dropping ADR here for review: short/long prompt-aware scheduling policy (two-queue + aging) for mixed workloads. Main goal: cut p95 tail without tanking throughput. Please focus on fairness + multi-tenant implications + gating requirements. Link: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-short-long-prompt-scheduling-policy

Lena Fischer: tl;dr: classify reqs as short/long based on prompt tokens + predicted prefill cost, keep two queues, dispatch biased toward short w/ token budget + aging so long makes progress. Feature-flagged, defaults off.

Architecture Bot: ADR review opened by Noah Patel. Requested reviewers: @architecture-core @eng-sre

Sofia Ivanova: For context: this is prototype phase only. Not trying to productize tenant QoS yet, but we need to not accidentally create one (or create unfairness that trips up Dedicated customers).

Sean Gallagher: SRE hat: I’m mostly worried about “silent policy change” in multi-tenant hosted pools. If we bias shorts, long-context tenants could see degraded p95/p99 and we won’t notice unless we split metrics by class + tenant. Needs strong guardrails.

Noah Patel: +1. ADR currently says “fairness guardrails” but I want it explicitly defined. What’s the fairness objective? Token fairness? Request fairness? Time-based? Right now it reads like: “try to keep shorts fast, ensure longs don’t starve” which is… not a policy, it’s a hope :slightly_smiling_face:

Lena Fischer: Fair call. For prototype we’re optimizing user-visible latency (TTFT/p95) for “short” while guaranteeing bounded waiting for long via aging. We’re not guaranteeing strict per-tenant fairness. We can add explicit invariants:
- starvation bound: max time-in-queue for long < X (config)
- dispatch share floor for long when long queue depth > 0
- cap on consecutive short dispatch iterations

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel side question: does class selection affect kernel auto-selection? We’ve had issues where mixing seq lengths causes kernel thrash. If policy oscillates between short/long every iteration, we could make that worse.

Lena Fischer: We’re planning token budgeting per “scheduler iteration” so we can batch multiple shorts before switching. Also config “min_run_length” for a class to avoid oscillation. But yes, kernel selection stability is a risk.

Noah Patel: Multi-tenant: even if not “QoS product”, if hosted pool has two classes, we’re implicitly prioritizing one traffic type over another. If a single tenant floods shorts, they can starve others’ longs unless aging is global not per-tenant. How are we defining aging scope?

Sofia Ivanova: Current implementation in PR draft is global per-pool queues. Aging is per-request based on enqueue time, not per-tenant. That helps starvation but still means a tenant with lots of shorts increases contention overall.

Paula Mendes: Product/rollout perspective: we *need* this gated per pool + per model, and ideally per customer tier in Dedicated. We also need a comms plan if Dedicated customers see long-context slowdowns.

Sean Gallagher: Gating requirements (IMO non-negotiable):
1) feature flag default OFF
2) allowlist pools (canary only)
3) runtime config bounds (no “set bias=1000 and melt GPU”)
4) metrics split by class + overall (p50/p95/p99, queue time, TTFT)
5) rollback is “flip flag” + revert params, no deploy required

Allison Grant: On metrics: we should align with existing naming conventions. Please don’t ship ad-hoc labels that explode cardinality. If you want tenant visibility, we need to do it via sampled traces or aggregation by “pool/model/class”, not “customer_id”.

Noah Patel: +1 on cardinality. ADR should explicitly say “no per-tenant labels”. But we still need to reason about tenant fairness without measuring it directly. Maybe define success criteria at pool level and watch for regressions in long-class p95.

Lena Fischer: Agree. Proposed instrumentation:
- scheduler_queue_depth{class=short|long}
- scheduler_time_in_queue_ms_bucket{class=...}
- scheduler_dispatch_share{class=...}
- scheduler_starvation_events_total{class=long}
- ttft_ms{class=...} (already exists, we add class label w/ bounded cardinality)

Sean Gallagher: Make sure “class” label is strictly {short,long} (and maybe “unknown”) and not a freeform tier.

Hiro Tanaka: Also please gate “prefill/decode separation hooks” behind the same flag. We don’t want the refactor to change behavior when policy is off.

Sofia Ivanova: Yes. Plan is: with flag off, code path should behave exactly like today’s queue selection. We’ll add regression tests around batch composition and ordering.

Noah Patel: Another concern: this looks like SRPT-lite. In multi-tenant systems SRPT can be unfair under certain distributions. ADR should include explicit statement: “We’re not implementing SRPT; we’re implementing two-class scheduling with bounded starvation.” And list failure modes.

Lena Fischer: Will update ADR with a section:
- Not SRPT (no continuous reordering by remaining tokens)
- Risks: long degradation, kernel thrash, cache eviction, oscillation
- Mitigations: token budgets, min-run, aging, caps

Connor O'Brien: Release perspective: if behavior changes even with flag off, we can’t land it right before runtime cut. Please keep “no-op when disabled” super tight.

Allison Grant: Re dashboards: we should require a pre-defined dashboard before canary. Otherwise SRE is blind. Can you add “dashboard exists” as a rollout prerequisite in ADR?

Sean Gallagher: Yes please. Also a rollback trigger: e.g. if overall p95 or long-class p95 increases > N% for > M minutes, auto page + flip flag.

Mateo Alvarez: Exec sponsor view: I’m okay taking some risk in canary as long as the blast radius is controlled. But I want the ADR to be crisp about what’s gated and what the “kill switch” is. And we should call out that “multi-tenant QoS is out of scope” explicitly so we don’t accidentally promise it.

Noah Patel: Summarizing required ADR edits from this thread:
- Define fairness objective + explicit invariants (starvation bound, dispatch floor/caps)
- Clarify aging scope (per-request, global queue)
- Explicit gating: flag default OFF, allowlist pools, per-model/pool params, bounded config
- “No-op when disabled” requirement (prefill/decode refactor included)
- Observability requirements: per-class metrics with bounded cardinality + dashboard prerequisite
- Rollback triggers + kill switch
- Explicitly not SRPT + enumerate failure modes

Lena Fischer: Ack. I’ll push an ADR revision today and tag reviewers. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Add me on the SRE sign-off for canary gating. Once metrics + dashboard spec are in, we can pre-approve the canary plan.

Allison Grant: Please loop me on metric names before you merge. I’ll sanity check against our conventions.

Hiro Tanaka: And loop kernels once you decide min-run/token budget defaults; we need to validate no kernel oscillation on A100/H100.

Noah Patel: Cool. Marking “changes requested” on ADR until the above is addressed.

Architecture Bot: Review status updated: changes requested by Noah Patel.

Connor O'Brien: Release perspective: if behavior changes even with flag off, we can’t land it right before runtime cut. Please keep “no-op when disabled” super tight.

Allison Grant: Re dashboards: we should require a pre-defined dashboard before canary. Otherwise SRE is blind. Can you add “dashboard exists” as a rollout prerequisite in ADR?

Sean Gallagher: Yes please. Also a rollback trigger: e.g. if overall p95 or long-class p95 increases > N% for > M minutes, auto page + flip flag.

Mateo Alvarez: Exec sponsor view: I’m okay taking some risk in canary as long as the blast radius is controlled. But I want the ADR to be crisp about what’s gated and what the “kill switch” is. And we should call out that “multi-tenant QoS is out of scope” explicitly so we don’t accidentally promise it.

Noah Patel: Summarizing required ADR edits from this thread:
- Define fairness objective + explicit invariants (starvation bound, dispatch floor/caps)
- Clarify aging scope (per-request, global queue)
- Explicit gating: flag default OFF, allowlist pools, per-model/pool params, bounded config
- “No-op when disabled” requirement (prefill/decode refactor included)
- Observability requirements: per-class metrics with bounded cardinality + dashboard prerequisite
- Rollback triggers + kill switch
- Explicitly not SRPT + enumerate failure modes

Lena Fischer: Ack. I’ll push an ADR revision today and tag reviewers. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Add me on the SRE sign-off for canary gating. Once metrics + dashboard spec are in, we can pre-approve the canary plan.

Allison Grant: Please loop me on metric names before you merge. I’ll sanity check against our conventions.

Hiro Tanaka: And loop kernels once you decide min-run/token budget defaults; we need to validate no kernel oscillation on A100/H100.

Noah Patel: Cool. Marking “changes requested” on ADR until the above is addressed.

Architecture Bot: Review status updated: changes requested by Noah Patel.

title: #architecture: 1768471900
preview:
Confluence Bot: ADR updated: ADR-042 Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-042

Miles Thornton: dropping this here for architecture review. main questions: what signals are authoritative enough to flip regions, and what guardrails we need to avoid oscillation. would love comments today/tomorrow so we can unblock ENG-2412.

Ravi Menon: skimmed. overall +1 on “reachability > 5xx burn > tail latency burn > capacity”. one nit: reachability needs to be *from where*? if it’s only from our control plane vantage points, we risk failing over for a bad ISP / partial internet issue.

Victor Alvarez: yeah, strongly agree. reachability is tricky. i’d recommend defining “authoritative reachability” as: (a) cloud internal (region-to-region) + (b) external synthetic probes from at least 2 providers/PoPs. if only one fails, it’s not authoritative.

Benji Okafor: +1. also, reachability should have a hold-down like “must be failing for N consecutive windows” AND ideally corroborate with data-plane symptoms (e.g., gateway 5xx burn or TLS handshake failures). otherwise we’ll get flip-flop when probes are flaky.

Allison Grant: on the measurement side: please be explicit which 5xx signal we treat as authoritative. app 5xx vs gateway 5xx vs upstream (model backend) errors. i’ve seen incidents where backend error spikes but gateway was fine due to retries masking it.

Jordan Lee: deterministic evaluation question: ADR says “take highest-priority signal that exceeds threshold.” what happens when reachability is “unknown” because telemetry stream is missing? defaulting unknown => unhealthy would be a disaster.

Miles Thornton: good call. my intent is unknown => “insufficient evidence”, not unhealthy. in that case the policy should fall back to next signal *only if* it’s corroborated by at least one independent signal.

Tomáš Novák: +1 on the “unknown handling” being spelled out. we’ve been paged by missing metrics before. would prefer “unknown never triggers failover” unless operator forces.

Sean Gallagher: i want to push on one thing: do we really want reachability to be highest? historically our biggest customer impact is sustained elevated 5xx + timeouts, not just probe failures. reachability probes can be noisy.

Victor Alvarez: if we don’t treat reachability as top-tier, we’ll fail over late in cases of real network partition where app metrics lag (because traffic can’t reach the region to generate 5xx). but we can tighten the definition so it’s less noisy.

Ravi Menon: maybe we separate “hard down” vs “degraded” modes? like: reachability-hard-fail => immediate flip (fast path), but reachability-soft / partial => needs corroboration with 5xx burn.

Mina Haddad: +1, also helps with partial outages. we’ve had cases where only one AZ had issues. if reachability is “region down” but it’s actually one LB path, we might move too much traffic and cause overload elsewhere.

Allison Grant: could encode this as levels: HEALTHY / DEGRADED / UNHEALTHY. reachability can drive UNHEALTHY only when (1) probe failure across >=2 PoPs AND (2) internal east-west also failing OR error burn is already elevated.

Jordan Lee: from policy-engine POV, would love it if ADR defines:
- signal “authoritativeness” (hard vs soft)
- required corroboration count
- minimum dwell time before a flip
- cooldown after flip
So we can implement deterministically and unit test.

Benji Okafor: on oscillation: the ADR currently has “dampening timers” but doesn’t say if they’re per-signal or per-action. i’d recommend:
1) per-signal debounce (e.g., 3/5 windows)
2) action hold-down (don’t flip back for X minutes unless catastrophic)
3) rate limit flips per tenant/tier (max 1 per 30m?)

Sergio Costa: +1. and log a reason code that’s stable (small enum) for dashboards. if we include raw threshold values in reason, cardinality will explode.

Miles Thornton: noted. reason codes will be like REACHABILITY_HARD_FAIL, ERROR_BURN_FAST, LATENCY_BURN_FAST, CAPACITY_EXHAUSTION, OPERATOR_OVERRIDE.

Sean Gallagher: can we make “capacity exhaustion” not an automatic failover trigger by default? we’ve seen headroom metrics lie during scale events. if we fail over on capacity signal alone, we could cascade.

Mina Haddad: agree. capacity should probably gate *how much* traffic we shift, not *whether* we shift. e.g. “if target region headroom < X, then partial failover + shed low-priority tiers.”

Benji Okafor: yes. i want explicit mention: “capacity is a safety gate and shaping input, not a primary trigger.” failover triggered by reachability/errors/latency; capacity decides blast radius.

Victor Alvarez: also: reachability failures can happen due to DNS / TTL / resolver cache weirdness. do we have a plan to distinguish “DNS wrong” vs “region down”? maybe include separate DNS health (authoritative resolvers + anycast).

Allison Grant: we can add a “network/DNS reachability” panel, but i’m wary of adding another top-tier signal without clarity. maybe fold into reachability bucket but require internal + external agreement.

Jordan Lee: for “prevent oscillation”: is rollback handled in ADR-043? if so, ADR-042 should at least reference the interface: i.e., this ADR decides *when to flip*, ADR-043 decides *when to revert*.

Miles Thornton: yep, ADR-043 is coming next. i’ll add a section in ADR-042: “this ADR does not define rollback; see ADR-043.” and will clarify unknown-handling + hard/soft reachability.

Sean Gallagher: request: include explicit operator mode guidance. e.g. “automation in prod requires corroboration >=2 signals” vs “operator override can flip with 1 signal.” we don’t want oncall to assume the system will do something it won’t.

Tomáš Novák: +1 and please document “do not fail over” scenarios. ex: single model backend regression in one region shouldn’t flip all Hosted API routes.

Ravi Menon: last point: tail latency burn can be a side effect of retry storms. if we trigger failover on latency burn, we might amplify. can we require latency burn + elevated 5xx OR latency burn + reachability degraded?

Miles Thornton: this is super helpful. action items i’m taking for ADR text:
- define reachability hard vs soft and required corroboration
- unknown signal semantics (unknown != unhealthy)
- capacity as safety gate/shaping, not standalone trigger
- oscillation: per-signal debounce + action hold-down + rate limits (with refs to ADR-043)
- add explicit operator vs automation corroboration rules
will push an updated draft EOD and re-tag.

Confluence Bot: Miles Thornton edited ADR-042 Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-042

Allison Grant: once it’s updated, ping me and i’ll sanity check the metrics definitions so we don’t bake in ambiguous “5xx” language.

Victor Alvarez: i’ll add a comment in-page with a concrete reachability criteria proposal (PoPs/providers/internal).

title: #architecture: 1768003300
preview:
Talia Benson: Hi folks — need architecture eyes on the Shadow Traffic routing ADR before we mark it “accepted”. Can you review and leave comments inline by EOD tomorrow? Main things we need clarity on: *where* the mirroring happens in the request path + expected perf impact.

Talia Benson: Confluence ADR draft: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-Shadow-Traffic-Routing-Architecture (WIP)

Ravi Menon: Read through. Overall direction makes sense (inline mirroring, fail-open). I think the ADR needs to be explicit about placement:
1) request enters router
2) auth/tenant resolution
3) rollout variant assignment (control)
4) return control response to user
5) mirror *in parallel* to shadow target(s)

Ravi Menon: Right now section 2.1 kind of implies we might mirror before variant assignment; that would make comparing control vs shadow messy.

Mina Haddad: +1 to Ravi. Also: mirroring should happen after request normalization (canonicalized request payload) so fingerprinting is stable + we don’t end up hashing raw headers w/ PII.

Priya Natarajan: Skimmed. I’m aligned on inline mirroring as default vs async replay, but the ADR needs stronger justification on *performance risk*. Specifically:
- additional CPU in the hot path (hashing + serialization)
- additional network fanout and connection pool pressure
- any extra allocations around streaming

Priya Natarajan: Can we quantify overhead targets in the ADR (even rough): e.g., p99 added router CPU < X%, added latency on user path ~0ms (since fire-and-forget), and hard drop conditions.

Ethan Park: We can add a perf budget section. Implementation plan we’re using is: sampling decision happens early, and if not sampled we do basically nothing besides compute fingerprint (which we already do for rollout bucketing).

Ethan Park: For sampled requests, we spawn an async task with strict limits (max inflight per route + global circuit breaker). User path never awaits the shadow call.

Sean Gallagher: SRE view: the ADR should explicitly state what “fail-open” means at runtime. e.g.
- shadow target call timeout: 1.5s (or lower)
- if shadow queue depth > N OR event ingestion lag > M -> disable shadowing automatically (circuit breaker)
- if router is under load -> shadowing is first thing to shed

Sean Gallagher: Also I’d like it to call out that shadow errors are “acceptable” up to some threshold without paging (ticket vs page), so folks don’t assume shadow 99.9% success is required.

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a note about metrics cardinality. If router emits per-request tags, we’ll blow up Prometheus/OTel. The ADR should reference the companion data model ADR and explicitly say we only emit low-card dimensions.

Caleb Johnson: On perf: if we do inline mirroring, do we plan to emit an event on the control path (request start/end) AND a separate shadow pair event later? Need clarity on how we join control+shadow without storing huge payloads in the router.

Jordan Lee: Re: join key, in the schema thread we agreed on a deterministic `request_fingerprint_v1` + a per-request `shadow_pair_id` (or request_id) that’s safe to emit. Suggest ADR says: join happens in telemetry pipeline, not in router memory.

Jordan Lee: Also the ADR should explicitly specify the hashing input. I don’t want “implementation-defined” here. Proposed:
- canonicalized request body (excluding headers)
- route_id + tenant_id
- HMAC w/ server secret
Then truncate to 128 bits.

Mina Haddad: +1 but be careful: request body can be huge (multi-message chat). We should specify we hash a bounded representation: e.g., stable hash over first N bytes + lengths + model params. Otherwise we create CPU spikes.

Ravi Menon: Agree. For bucketing we don’t need cryptographic perfection, just stable-enough and privacy safe. HMAC is good. Also call out collision expectations and that collisions just add noise, not correctness bugs.

Benji Okafor: FYI we already made some policy decisions in eng-platform (schema review). Shadow policy defaults to consistent hashing using `request_fingerprint_v1`; no custom hash keys. Fail-open only (v1). Default `max_inflight_per_route=100` server-side. Might be useful to incorporate those into the ADR so it doesn’t read like “anything goes”.

Priya Natarajan: Great. Another question: where exactly is the mirror executed? In the same router process? separate threadpool? sidecar? The ADR says “routing layer” but that’s ambiguous.

Ethan Park: Current plan is router process (same binary), but separate “shadow executor” threadpool + bounded queue. When queue is full we drop. No backpressure to user path.

Sean Gallagher: Please spell out: bounded queue, bounded concurrency, and “drop” semantics (including counters). Without that, folks will keep adding retries and we’ll self-DDOS.

Allison Grant: Also: we should state whether the shadow call uses the same egress path and connection pools as the user call. If shared, we need a per-pool cap so shadowing doesn’t starve the control traffic under ephemeral port pressure.

Ravi Menon: Good call. ADR can say: shadow egress uses a dedicated client with lower priority + smaller pool + separate timeouts.

Caleb Johnson: Streaming: do we mirror streaming requests at all? If we do “mirror full prompt at start” that’s fine, but mirroring the stream tokens might double traffic and create weird partial captures.

Mina Haddad: For v1, I think the ADR should say: we mirror the *input* (request body) and capture the *shadow output* as non-streaming in the background (i.e., worker consumes full response). For user path, we keep streaming unaffected.

Jordan Lee: There’s still the issue that some request types depend on streaming partial tool calls, etc. But for shadow eval we mostly need final output. We can be explicit that shadow capture may be truncated/partial under timeouts and scoring must handle “incomplete”.

Priya Natarajan: +1: make degraded behavior explicit. The ADR should say what happens when:
- shadow target returns 429/5xx
- shadow target exceeds timeout
- scoring pipeline is down
- telemetry ingestion is lagging

Sean Gallagher: And importantly: none of those should page oncall unless control path is impacted. Shadow path pages should be separate and higher threshold.

Ethan Park: Action items I’m hearing for ADR:
- add explicit placement diagram (after control variant selection)
- define perf budget + overhead expectations
- define executor model (bounded queue, bounded concurrency, drop policy)
- define deterministic fingerprint input + bounded hashing
- define streaming semantics (v1: input mirrored, shadow output captured best-effort)
- clarify egress isolation (separate pool)
- add failure mode table w/ behavior and metrics

Ravi Menon: Yep. Also suggest we add a short section “Why not async replay from logs?” with the key point: replay requires retaining prompts longer + creates selection bias + not real-time for canary decisions.

Allison Grant: Please reference the data-model/cardinality ADR in the routing ADR (even just a link + one paragraph). Otherwise folks will invent dimensions and we’ll pay later.

Benji Okafor: I’ll paste the schema constraints into the ADR comments (max targets, fail-open only, header allowlist/denylist) so it stays consistent.

Priya Natarajan: Sounds good. Once those are incorporated I’m comfortable approving. I’ll re-review tomorrow.

Talia Benson: Thanks all. Ethan/Ravi/Mina can you take the ADR edits? Please tag @Priya Natarajan and me when the revised draft is ready, and we’ll move it to “Accepted” if there are no remaining blockers. :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: Acknowledged. Will push updates and ping.

title: #architecture: 1767579112
preview:
ArchReviewBot: New architecture review request posted by @Omar El-Sayed. Please review within 48h. Links: Confluence (design) https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/kv-compaction , Draft ADRs https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-compaction-budget + https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-eviction-policy

Omar El-Sayed: Hey folks, requesting arch review feedback on KV compaction allocator changes. Main asks:
1) allocator invariants we should codify (what must never be violated)
2) correctness boundaries for relocation (what we guarantee vs what scheduler must guarantee)
3) rollback plan / kill-switch safety (what happens mid-relocation)

PR is still WIP but design is pretty stable. @Noah Patel can you take first pass?

Noah Patel: Yup. Skimming now. Quick clarifying q: are we allowing relocation of *active* sequences (in decode) or only “paused at boundary” sequences?

Samir Qureshi: Current proposal: relocation only when seq enters “movable” state at scheduler boundary (prefill->decode boundary or between decode steps). We don’t relocate while a decode kernel is reading KV.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. Scheduler hook will mark a batch boundary. We can do it after token emission / before next decode step. For continuous batching, we treat “step” as the scheduling quantum.

Noah Patel: Good. Then the core correctness question becomes: what is the exact handshake between scheduler and allocator during that boundary? Need a crisp invariant like “no kernel holds pointers into KV arena during relocation”. How is that enforced? (epoch, refcount, lock, etc)

Samir Qureshi: Mechanism is per-seq state + epoch barrier. Scheduler sets seq.state = MOVING and increments kv_epoch; decode path checks epoch at step start. We also take a short allocator lock for page table swap.

Noah Patel: Please write the invariants explicitly in doc/ADR. Something like:
- I1: For a given sequence, its KV mapping is only mutated while seq.state ∈ {MOVING, EVICTING}.
- I2: A seq can enter MOVING only when it is not present in any in-flight batch (no outstanding GPU work referencing its KV).
- I3: After relocation, mapping swap is atomic wrt readers at step boundary (single pointer swap / generation bump).

Also define what “not present in any in-flight batch” means in your runtime (CPU-side queue vs GPU completion).

Julian Moore: +1 on “GPU completion” nuance. Our trace spans today show decode launch->completion can overlap next scheduler tick for other seqs. Need explicit sync point for the moving seq.

Sofia Ivanova: Implementation detail: we already have per-batch CUDA event tracking for streaming. For MOVING seq we’ll wait on its last decode event (not global sync). That’s in ENG-4824 notes.

Noah Patel: Great. Then I’d like to see a statement: “Relocation requires waiting on last_decode_event(seq)”. Put that in correctness boundary.

Omar El-Sayed: Makes sense. We’ll add it to design + tests.

Noah Patel: Next: allocator invariants.
What’s the unit of allocation? pages/blocks? And are we guaranteeing no partial block moves?

Samir Qureshi: Arena is split into fixed-size pages (currently 64KB). Seqs have a page list per layer/head. Compaction moves whole pages and rewrites the mapping table. We never move partial pages.

Noah Patel: Then add:
- I4: A page is owned by exactly one seq at a time OR in free list; no shared pages.
- I5: Free list correctness: a page can appear in free list at most once; transitions are linearizable.
- I6: Mapping table is source of truth; any cached pointer must be derivable + invalidated on epoch bump.

Also please define what happens with “prefix cache” pages if applicable. Are they under same allocator? (I saw mention of cached prefixes being preemptible.)

Omar El-Sayed: Prefix cache uses same arena but separate namespace/owner type. We tagged those pages as owner=PREFIX_CACHE and have separate eviction priority. Compaction can move them too, but eviction can drop them.

Lena Fischer: On correctness boundaries: eviction of prefix cache is fine, eviction of active sequences is not (unless scheduler cancels seq). We should be explicit that “eviction” for sequences = “preempt” which is a higher-level decision.

Noah Patel: Exactly. Language matters. I’d suggest:
- “Eviction” only applies to cacheable artifacts (prefix cache, speculative prefill results)
- “Preemption” applies to sequences, and requires scheduler-level cancellation semantics + client-visible behavior

Do you ever “evict” a sequence’s KV to CPU? If not, keep it out of scope.

Omar El-Sayed: Not in this phase. Only drop prefix cache / low-priority cached artifacts. Seqs are either kept resident or rejected at admission.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 keep it narrow. If we say “evict sequences” it’ll get misread as swap-to-host.

Allison Grant: Observability angle: please also define invariant around metrics emission during rollback. If kill-switch flips mid-move, what metrics do we expect? (don’t want half-updated counters)

Samir Qureshi: Counters are per-step. We increment “bytes_moved” after successful mapping swap + verification. If abort, we record “compaction_aborts_total” and don’t add bytes moved.

Noah Patel: Good. On rollback plan / kill-switch:
- Can kill-switch be flipped at runtime without restart?
- What happens if it’s flipped while a seq is MOVING?

Omar El-Sayed: Kill-switch is config propagated via control-plane; runtime reads it every N seconds (default 5s). Behavior:
- If disabled, scheduler stops selecting new relocation work.
- If a relocation is in progress, we complete the current “page copy + swap” step then stop (don’t start a new step).

Noah Patel: That’s sane. But you need to articulate the state machine so “stop” doesn’t leave dangling pages.
Propose explicit guarantee:
- G1: At any point during relocation, there exists exactly one valid mapping (either old or new). We do not publish the new mapping until copy is complete.
- G2: If disabled mid-step, allocator reverts to old mapping (or continues to new) but never leaves mixed mapping.

How do you prevent partial publish? (I assume a single mapping pointer swap.)

Samir Qureshi: Yes, mapping swap is a single atomic pointer update to per-seq page table + generation bump. Copy occurs into fresh pages, then we flip. Only after flip we free old pages.

Julian Moore: Do we have a post-copy verification? (like checksum?) Probably too expensive, but maybe a debug mode.

Samir Qureshi: In debug builds we can optionally run a lightweight sanity check (page table sizes + bounds) and poison old pages before free. Not checksumming content.

Noah Patel: That’s fine; document it as “debug-only guardrail”.

Sofia Ivanova: For scheduler boundary, we also need to mention we won’t relocate if the seq is in “STREAMING_PARTIAL” state (client is mid-stream) unless we have the event barrier. But we do have per-seq event barrier, so it should be ok. Still, I’ll add a note that relocation is disallowed when we can’t attach an event (legacy path).

Noah Patel: Please call that out as a correctness boundary: “compaction requires CUDA event tracking enabled”. If there are fallback codepaths, they should opt-out.

Lena Fischer: Backpressure integration: if fragmentation high and compaction budget exhausted, we may still OOM. What’s the policy? We should ensure admission control triggers before allocator hard OOM.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposed: if frag_ratio > threshold AND free_pages < min_free_pages, we signal scheduler “KV pressure” which tightens admission and can trigger prefix cache eviction first. Compaction is best-effort, not the only safety.

Noah Patel: Good. Make that explicit under “correctness boundaries”: compaction is not relied on for safety; admission control remains primary OOM prevention.

Allison Grant: On rollout/rollback criteria: do we have a hard metric-based rollback? (p99 spike correlated with compaction time)

Omar El-Sayed: Yes, SRE is defining it, but initial suggestion: rollback if p99 decode latency +15% for 10m AND compaction_time_ms_p99 > budget AND eviction_rate > threshold. Still tuning.

Mateo Alvarez: We’ll put exact thresholds in SRE guardrails doc. Design doc should just state: “feature flagged, canary, auto-rollback wired to tail latency + compaction metrics.”

Noah Patel: Last request: tests.
Please add a stress test that hits cancellation during relocation (classic hazard). Even if scheduler sets MOVING only at boundaries, cancellations can happen asynchronously.

Samir Qureshi: Already on the list (ENG-4827). We’ll model: cancel arrives after copy but before mapping swap; and cancel after swap but before freeing old pages.

Noah Patel: Perfect. Summarizing review feedback:
- Write explicit invariants I1-I6 + guarantees G1-G2 in doc
- Define boundary conditions: requires per-seq GPU completion event; opt-out legacy paths
- Clarify vocabulary: eviction=cache artifacts; sequences are never evicted (only admitted/rejected/cancelled)
- Kill-switch semantics: stop selecting new work; finish current step; never publish partial mapping
- Add cancellation stress tests around swap/free window

Once those are in, I’m comfortable with the architecture direction.

Omar El-Sayed: Awesome, thanks. I’ll update the Confluence page + ADR text and ping back for final sign-off.

Noah Patel: Sounds good. :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: +1, please tag me when metric definitions are finalized so we can align names with the standard.

Omar El-Sayed: Will do.

title: #architecture: 1763894410
preview:
Noah Patel: Dropping ADR draft here for final nitpicks before I mark “in review” -> “accepted”: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/ADR-override-order

Noah Patel: TL;DR: how we ship the hardware tuning profile pack + override precedence (fleet defaults vs per-pool vs per-deploy vs per-route/customer), plus rollback semantics. Would love eyes from Infra/SRE + anyone who’s been burned by config drift.

Ethan Park: skimming now. first nit: the precedence list in section 3 reads like “later wins” but then section 4 implies “fleet defaults override customer overrides for safety”. those are contradictory.

Ethan Park: can we explicitly state: (a) which layers are allowed to override which keys (key-level allowlist), and (b) whether safety clamps are applied after merge. otherwise readers will assume you can override anything via env var.

Benji Okafor: +1. I also think “env vars highest precedence” is too blunt. We’ve been using env as emergency toggle, but in Dedicated/Private it becomes a footgun (random helm chart sets env, overrides everything silently).

Benji Okafor: Suggest: env vars only for a small set of debug/kill-switch knobs (e.g. DISABLE_PROFILE_PACK, FORCE_PROFILE_ID), not arbitrary scheduler settings.

Diego Santos: from deployment compatibility angle: Hosted will use control-plane pushed config, Dedicated/Private via helm values + config file mount. if we keep “env always wins”, we’re creating different behavior across modes b/c not all modes have env injection in the same place.

Diego Santos: we should define override sources in a way that maps cleanly:
- runtime baked defaults
- packaged profile pack
- external profile bundle (optional)
- deployment config (control plane / helm values)
- request-level overrides (route/customer)
- emergency toggles
and then specify priority.

Noah Patel: good call. I was trying to keep it simple but yeah, it reads inconsistent.

Quentin Harris: another nit: the ADR says “profile version pinned per deployment” but doesn’t say what happens if runtime doesn’t support that profile version.

Quentin Harris: we need to specify compatibility check behavior: fail hard vs warn + fallback. for Private, fail hard might brick a cluster during upgrade; for Hosted, failing hard is scary too.

Neha Kapoor: +1 please don’t “fail hard” by default. SRE preference: startup should be conservative:
- if explicit override requested (FORCE_PROFILE_ID/profile_version pinned) and incompatible -> fail fast (user asked for it)
- if auto-selected profile incompatible -> log loudly + fall back to safe baseline profile (or runtime defaults)

Neha Kapoor: also want rollback semantics called out: “disable profile pack for pool X” should be reversible without redeploy (i.e. control plane flag / config map change). ADR currently reads like “ship inside runtime binary” which makes rollback = new runtime deploy.

Irene Choi: infra side: we can do both, but we have to say what’s default. baked-in pack is good for Private/offline installs, but Hosted needs a way to hotfix profiles without waiting for runtime release train.

Irene Choi: maybe state: “runtime includes last-known-good pack; control plane can optionally mount/serve newer pack; selection chooses external pack if signed + compatible.”

Connor O'Brien: release train concern: if ADR encourages “external bundle can override baked pack”, we need a clear ownership + signoff gate. otherwise teams will bypass runtime release process.

Connor O'Brien: ask: who is allowed to publish external pack versions? Runtime kernels/scheduling only? and do we require perf-canary green on all SKUs before promotion?

Paula Mendes: from canary/rollback requirements: please add explicit “blast radius” notes. e.g.
- default path is “enable new pack in canary pool only”
- rollback trigger thresholds (p95, error rate, OOM) should flip DISABLE_PROFILE_PACK at pool scope
- per-customer override should never be the first rollback lever during incident (too slow)

Marissa Cole: product/UX nit: ADR says “per-route override wins” but we should be careful: customers will expect per-route config to be stable even if fleet defaults change.

Marissa Cole: can we state “customer-visible overrides are sticky + audited” and that we’ll expose “effective profile + source” in console? otherwise Support is going to get murdered.

Benji Okafor: config drift risk: ADR should explicitly mention “normalized defaults” problem.

Benji Okafor: right now we have runtime hard-coded defaults + profile pack defaults. if both exist, people change one and forget the other -> drift. proposal: runtime hard-coded defaults are minimal safe baseline; all tuning lives in pack; runtime logs effective config at boot.

Ethan Park: yes pls. ADR should require:
1) single source of truth for default values (pack)
2) runtime prints “effective config” (or at least key diffs) + profile id/version
3) validation rejects unknown keys (to prevent silent no-ops)

Diego Santos: also “override order” should be deterministic and observable. i.e. metrics tag: selected_profile_id, selected_profile_version, override_source (auto/bundle/control-plane/helm/env).

Neha Kapoor: +1000 on observability. and please call out that we need an oncall-friendly toggle:
- disable profile selection entirely (fallback to baseline)
- disable only external bundle, keep baked-in
- force baseline profile (per pool)

Noah Patel: ok, proposed edits based on this thread:
- rewrite precedence section as an ordered list with “who can override what” (key allowlist + safety clamps applied last)
- split “env vars” into “emergency toggles only”
- add compatibility behavior matrix (explicit override vs auto-select)
- clarify distribution: baked-in LKG pack + optional externally served pack with signoff
- add config drift mitigation requirements: single source of truth + startup logs + effective-config dump + metrics

Noah Patel: I’ll push a revision today and tag you all. Anything else blocking acceptance?

Quentin Harris: one more nit: “per-customer override” should be clearly separated from “per-route override” in ADR language. per-customer might apply across multiple routes + models; per-route is more granular. readers will conflate.

Irene Choi: +1. also mention GPU SKU detection variability (string variants) -> if detection ambiguous, we should prefer baseline rather than guessing and picking wrong profile.

Paula Mendes: not blocking, but please include “roll forward vs rollback” guidance: if pack is bad, disable quickly; if only one knob is bad, prefer hotfix in external bundle + canary before re-enabling.

Connor O'Brien: as long as external bundle path has a clear promotion gate + ownership, I’m good.

Neha Kapoor: same. make sure ADR doesn’t sound like “expected behavior == guarantee” re perf. :thumbsup:

Noah Patel: noted. will update wording to “expected behavior / safe-by-default” and tie guarantees to SLO docs not ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. also please add a note that console will show “effective profile” but doesn’t promise a fixed latency number.

Noah Patel: cool. pushing update. thanks all.

- clarify distribution: baked-in LKG pack + optional externally served pack with signoff
- add config drift mitigation requirements: single source of truth + startup logs + effective-config dump + metrics

Noah Patel: I’ll push a revision today and tag you all. Anything else blocking acceptance?

Quentin Harris: one more nit: “per-customer override” should be clearly separated from “per-route override” in ADR language. per-customer might apply across multiple routes + models; per-route is more granular. readers will conflate.

Irene Choi: +1. also mention GPU SKU detection variability (string variants) -> if detection ambiguous, we should prefer baseline rather than guessing and picking wrong profile.

Paula Mendes: not blocking, but please include “roll forward vs rollback” guidance: if pack is bad, disable quickly; if only one knob is bad, prefer hotfix in external bundle + canary before re-enabling.

Connor O'Brien: as long as external bundle path has a clear promotion gate + ownership, I’m good.

Neha Kapoor: same. make sure ADR doesn’t sound like “expected behavior == guarantee” re perf. :thumbsup:

Noah Patel: noted. will update wording to “expected behavior / safe-by-default” and tie guarantees to SLO docs not ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. also please add a note that console will show “effective profile” but doesn’t promise a fixed latency number.

Noah Patel: cool. pushing update. thanks all.

title: #architecture: 1763335520
preview:
Claire Dubois: Dropping ADR-0172 draft here for review (metrics + run manifest schema). Would love eyes on: schema fields, versioning, and how we encode workload params + env capture without making it impossible to diff / store. Doc: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/ADR-0172-draft

Claire Dubois: Current proposal is 2 JSON artifacts per run:
1) `run_manifest.json` (inputs + env)
2) `results.json` (metrics + aggregations)

Claire Dubois: Biggest open questions called out in ADR:
- Do we version the schema (semver) vs a date stamp?
- Workload params: flatten vs nested (e.g., sequence length bins / distributions)
- Env capture: how strict is “required” (driver/CUDA, clocks, container digest, git SHA, region, etc.)

Rina Kobayashi: +1 on splitting manifest vs results. For harness it’s way easier to treat manifest as immutable. But we need to be careful that results references manifest by hash or filename, otherwise dashboards get messy.

Allison Grant: Reading. Two immediate comments:
1) metrics definitions need to be explicit about boundary (server-only vs client-observed). We already have confusion in SLO land.
2) env capture: please include “measurement boundary” fields too, not just driver versions.

Sean Gallagher: For env capture, we should distinguish “must for reproducibility” vs “nice to have”. Otherwise CI is going to fail constantly when a field is missing on some runner.

Noah Patel: On schema versioning: I’d prefer `schema_version: 1` + `schema_revision` semver-ish. Date stamp is ambiguous (and people will cargo-cult it).

Mateo Alvarez: Agree w/ Noah. Also: if we’re going to gate releases on this, we need backwards compatibility story. E.g., runtime adds new scheduler knob → manifests should still validate.

Jonas Weber: For workload params: pls keep them structured, not flattened. If we flatten everything into `workload_param_*` it’ll be unreadable. But we should be consistent about naming and units.

Logan Wright: Dashboarding/data pipeline angle: nested is ok as long as we have a stable path for key fields we group by. E.g. `model.name`, `hardware.gpu_sku`, `region`, `workload.id`, `workload.mode` should be top-level or at least fixed paths.

Connor O'Brien: On CI gating: validator should allow “unknown fields” so we can extend without breaking old runners. But we should lock down required fields.

Sahana Iyer: +1 and please keep the schema compatible with GitHub Actions artifacts size constraints. If env capture includes giant dumps it will blow up. (Put big stuff in separate artifacts and reference by URL/path.)

Claire Dubois: ok, let’s go point by point. Starting w/ versioning: ADR currently proposes
- `manifest_schema_version`: "0.1.0" (semver)
- `results_schema_version`: "0.1.0"
- `workload_suite_version`: "2026q1.v1" (string)
Feedback: are we ok with semver strings? Or do we want integer + changelog?

Noah Patel: Semver strings are fine, but define what increments mean. E.g. major = breaking validation/backwards incompatible, minor = new optional fields, patch = doc/typo.

Allison Grant: +1. And define a migration rule: when we bump major, we need a converter script in repo (even if best-effort).

Rina Kobayashi: From harness: semver string is easiest. We can embed it in filenames too (`run_manifest.v0.1.0.json`) but not required.

Sean Gallagher: Please keep schema versions independent from workload suite versions. Workload suite will change often; schema shouldn’t.

Claire Dubois: Next: workload params. Current sketch:
```
"workload": {
  "id": "chat_streaming",
  "variant": "prod_mix_v1",
  "mode": "streaming",
  "seed": 12345,
  "request_mix": {
    "chat": 0.65,
    "tool": 0.20,
    "summarize": 0.10,
    "other": 0.05
  },
  "distributions": {
    "prompt_tokens": {"p50": 180, "p90": 900, "max": 4096},
    "output_tokens": {"p50": 220, "p90": 800, "max": 2048}
  },
  "concurrency": {"target": 64, "ramp_seconds": 90}
}
```
Question: do we allow arbitrary keys under `distributions`? Or do we standardize on bins/histograms only?

Jonas Weber: For representativeness we often need bins, not just p50/p90/max. p50/p90 hides multimodal mixes. I’d propose we support two forms:
- `histogram_bins`: explicit bin edges + weights
- `summary`: p50/p90/max for quick glance

Logan Wright: From pipeline, histogram bins are fine but please keep the structure consistent. Something like:
`"histogram": {"bins": "0-32,33-64,...", "weights": "0.1,0.2,..."}` is ugly. Better:
```
"prompt_tokens": {
  "type": "histogram",
  "bins": "0-64;65-128;129-256",
  "weights": "0.12;0.34;0.54"
}
```
But I know ADR wants JSON not CSV strings…

Allison Grant: We can keep arrays in JSON but remember our file generation constraints? (Wait—this is internal, arrays are fine in the manifest itself. The Slack-exported file is separate.)

Rina Kobayashi: +1 arrays are fine in the manifest in GitHub. In this thread we’re just agreeing on structure.

Mateo Alvarez: Standardize what matters to perf: prompt token distribution, output token distribution, and arrival process. Everything else optional.

Sean Gallagher: Also need to specify caching scenario. There was a whole thread about cold vs warm. I’d like explicit:
- `cache_mode: cold|warm_prefix|warm_kv` (or similar)
Otherwise results are incomparable.

Connor O'Brien: +1, and if cache_mode impacts gating thresholds it must be a tag in results.

Claire Dubois: Great call. ADR has a footnote but not explicit field. Adding `cache_mode`.

Claire Dubois: Now env capture. Proposed required:
- runtime git SHA + build id
- container image digest
- region + cluster
- GPU sku + count
- driver version
- CUDA version
- runtime flags (scheduler policy id)

Optional:
- GPU clocks locked? (yes/no + value)
- NUMA / CPU pinning
- kernel selection dump path
- tracing artifacts paths

Sean Gallagher: I’d push GPU clocks + power settings into “required” for baseline runs at least. Otherwise variance will be huge and we’ll waste weeks. It can be optional for developer local runs.

Sahana Iyer: How do we encode “required for baseline runs” vs “optional for local”? maybe `run_type: baseline|canary|local` and validator enforces more fields based on run_type.

Allison Grant: Please don’t make validator super complex. But we can do conditional requirements with JSON schema `if/then`. That’s acceptable.

Noah Patel: +1. Keep it simple: `run_intent: baseline` triggers strictness.

Rina Kobayashi: I can implement conditional schema checks in the validator easily (we’re using ajv). We already do this for “streaming requires stream metrics”.

Jonas Weber: Also for env capture: include model config that affects quality/perf: quantization profile, max context, tensor parallel degree, dtype, kv cache dtype, etc.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 and scheduler knobs. But we need a line somewhere; otherwise manifest becomes a dump of every config option. Maybe:
- `config_fingerprint`: hash of full config
- `config_effective`: include only the subset that impacts perf (documented)

Logan Wright: Hash is helpful for de-duping. But dashboards need human-readable fields. So include both hash + a curated set.

Claire Dubois: ok. Drafting: `effective_config` section with curated keys + `full_config_ref` as artifact link.

Allison Grant: Make sure the curated keys include measurement boundary, like:
- `ttft_definition`: `server_first_token_generated` vs `first_byte_on_wire`
- `tpot_definition`: `server_token_interval` (exclude client read)
If we don’t record that, people will “fix” bugs and accidentally change definitions.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. In harness we can set those constants and write them into manifest.

Connor O'Brien: For results: propose we output both raw samples (maybe capped) + aggregated percentiles? Or only percentiles?

Sean Gallagher: For gating, we need percentiles. For triage, raw samples help a lot (to see multimodal). But raw samples get big.

Sahana Iyer: Could do: results has percentiles + a separate `samples.parquet` artifact. Results includes URL. Keep JSON small.

Claire Dubois: Works. ADR already suggests results JSON should be <= ~1MB.

Noah Patel: Another subtlety: multi-model fallback. If the runtime falls back to another kernel/model variant, we should record that. Otherwise baseline might accidentally include fallback path.

Mateo Alvarez: That’s in PR-21519 (kernel selection dump). In manifest we can include `kernel_selection: {"mode": "record", "sample_rate": 0.05}` + results include summary counts.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a results field like `kernel_fallback_rate` and `top_fallback_reasons`. That will help catch silent regressions.

Allison Grant: Careful: “top reasons” is a list. But for JSON results, list is fine. For dashboards we might need it as tags? Might be too high-cardinality. Maybe keep it as a drilldown link.

Logan Wright: +1. High-cardinality reasons will explode metrics. Put detailed reasons in artifact, keep a single scalar in results (e.g., fallback_rate) and a boolean `fallback_occurred`.

Claire Dubois: Summarizing decisions from this thread:
- Versioning: semver strings for manifest/results schema. Define major/minor/patch semantics + provide converter for major bumps.
- Workload params: structured `workload` object; support histograms (bins + weights) AND summary stats; add explicit `cache_mode`.
- Env capture: add `run_intent` to allow conditional required fields; baseline intent requires clock/power settings.
- Config: include curated `effective_config` + `config_fingerprint` + reference to full config artifact.
- Measurement boundary: record TTFT/TPOT definitions in manifest.
- Results: percentiles in JSON; raw samples in separate artifact.

Claire Dubois: I’ll update ADR text + schema sketch accordingly today. If anyone objects, please reply in-thread by EOD tomorrow.

Sean Gallagher: Looks good. Please ping me when you add the baseline strictness part, I want to make sure we capture the exact fields SRE cares about (clocks, placement, isolation).

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a short section in ADR for “metrics tag cardinality guidelines” so people don’t try to put fallback reasons as tags.

Rina Kobayashi: ack. I’ll take an action item to update validator once ADR lands. (PR-21432 will need a follow-up.)

Connor O'Brien: Thanks. Once schema is stable-ish, I’ll wire the version fields into perf-canary gating config so we can refuse unknown majors.

Claire Dubois: Perfect. Closing loop after edits.
