title: #architecture: 1762231191
preview:
Ravi Menon: dropping the WIP ADR/design link here for review (enforcement points + policy eval contract). would love eyes esp on trade-offs + auditability: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/hot-route-capacity-protection-adr-wip

Ravi Menon: key question: where do we enforce tier-aware protection? API gateway vs routing service vs runtime scheduler (or layered). constraints: low latency overhead, deterministic/explainable decisions for enterprise escalations, no new cascading failure mode.

Ethan Park: from control plane view, I’m leaning layered:
- gateway: static-ish rate limits/quotas + consistent 429 schema
- routing: admission control when we have fleet pressure signals (queue depth / gpu saturation)
- runtime: last-ditch backpressure to avoid total collapse
biggest risk is adding per-request policy network hop.

Vikram Singh: +1 on layered, but we need to be super clear what executes on the hot path. if gateway calls policy-engine over RPC per request, we’re dead.
what’s the expected overhead target? i’d say <= ~1ms p50 added, and importantly no p99 blowups under pressure.

Jordan Lee: policy eval plan in the draft is “local eval w/ cached policy snapshot”, not synchronous RPC.
- control plane publishes policy bundles (signed) to gateway + routing
- deterministic evaluation in-process
- decision log emitted async (sampling configurable)

auditability: we can guarantee “same inputs => same output” via versioned bundles + stable rules.

Amara Diallo: from gateway side: yes please keep it in-process. we can load the bundle into the existing limiter module.
also we need consistent 429 reasons:
- quota exceeded (window)
- rate limit exceeded (burst)
- overload shedding (admission)
- upstream backpressure (runtime)
if we mix 429/503 without clear semantics, support is going to hate us.

Allison Grant: on metrics/tags: do NOT tag by customer_id in the primary counters. we already got burned w cardinality.
route_group + tier + decision_reason + region should be enough for dashboards.
for audit, log the customer/tenant details in a separate sampled structured log stream (or on-demand query) rather than in metrics.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: tier attribution can’t come from client headers. needs to be derived from auth context (API key -> tenant -> contract tier) and then passed through as signed internal context.
if routing is doing admission based on tier, make sure it can’t be spoofed.

Peter Holtz: concern w “routing does admission”: when routing is degraded (or policy bundle stale), do we fail open or fail closed?
for enterprise SLO protection, i’d rather fail closed for best-effort and fail open for enterprise (i.e., preserve enterprise capacity even if policy eval is busted).
need explicit fallback behavior in ADR.

Mina Haddad: routing angle:
- we can gate best-effort first once queue depth crosses threshold
- we can also redirect best-effort to alternate pools/regions (when allowed)
- enterprise routes: prefer strict admission vs silent fallback unless product signs off (quality changes)
question: do we allow model fallback for /v1/chat/completions enterprise? or only for embeddings/rerank?

Rafael Mendes: SRE asks:
1) how do we debug “why was I 429’d” on-call? need a clear reason code + linkable decision context.
2) if admission control flips on due to pressure, we need an event + annotation on dashboards.
3) runbook should say: check queue depth, shed rate by tier, and which component is shedding (gateway vs routing vs runtime).

Sofia Ivanova: runtime perspective: runtime backpressure is necessary but we should avoid making it the primary enforcement. by the time runtime sees it, latency is already bad.
we can export scheduler_lag + queue_depth and also expose a “reject new work” signal upstream, but that needs to be coarse to avoid thrash.

Vikram Singh: +1. runtime shedding should be the circuit breaker, not the policy engine.
also: please define “capacity signal contract” (names, update cadence, smoothing). if routing is reading a noisy signal, we’ll oscillate and create weird tail latency.

Jordan Lee: auditability details:
- every decision includes: policy_bundle_version, route_group_id, tier, decision (allow/throttle/shed), reason_code
- include a “decision_id” hash over inputs so support can reference it
- store full input tuple in sampled logs only (request_id, tenant_id, route, model, limits)

Amara Diallo: for headers in 429, proposal:
- `x-redwood-retry-after-ms`
- `x-redwood-limit-reason` (enum)
- `x-redwood-policy-version`
body includes same fields + human copy.
need to confirm if we can return retry-after for overload shedding (sometimes answer is “don’t retry immediately”).

Allison Grant: pls avoid per-request “retry-after” that changes wildly; we should bucket/smooth it. otherwise clients retry in lockstep.
maybe return `retry-after` only for quota/rate limits; for overload return a fixed backoff suggestion.

Peter Holtz: on fail open/closed: i want crisp:
- if policy bundle missing/invalid: gateway defaults to existing global limits (safe), routing defaults to “protect enterprise” (shed best-effort earlier)
- if capacity signals missing: routing should degrade to static per-tier budgets, no fancy shaping.

Benji Okafor: rollout/ops notes:
- feature flag at gateway + routing separately
- allowlist enterprise tenants first
- we need rollback switch that *immediately* disables routing admission (since that’s most likely to surprise customers)
- policy bundle version should be pinned per region so we can roll forward/back.

Mina Haddad: question on latency overhead: if gateway does quota + rate check + policy eval, what’s the per-request CPU cost? we should benchmark.
Vikram can we get an agreed budget? (i’ll add to design acceptance criteria)

Vikram Singh: budget suggestion:
- added gateway overhead: p50 < 0.5ms, p99 < 2ms
- routing admission check: should be constant-time and in-process (no extra store read per request)
anything requiring redis roundtrips is out.

Ravi Menon: this is super helpful. i’ll update ADR with:
- layered enforcement (gateway quotas/rate + routing admission + runtime circuit breaker)
- explicit fail open/closed behavior (protect enterprise by default)
- audit log vs metrics cardinality split
- latency budgets + “no synchronous RPC” rule

Rafael Mendes: can we also add an internal debug endpoint spec somewhere? on-call will need a way to ask “given tenant X + route Y, what would policy do?” without tailing logs for an hour.

Jordan Lee: we have a draft for a restricted “policy explain” endpoint (admin-only). will add to ADR as follow-up item; needs authz + audit trail.

Amara Diallo: +1. support asks for “explain 429” all the time. even a console-only tool would help.

Allison Grant: make sure any explain endpoint is rate limited + audited; we don’t want it to become another hot path.

Ravi Menon: action items i’m capturing:
- (Ravi) update ADR w the above + post in #architecture for final sign-off
- (Jordan) add concrete audit fields + sampling guidance
- (Amara) propose final 429 reason codes + headers
- (Allison/Rafael) clarify metrics vs logs + dashboard annotations

Ravi Menon: will repost revised draft tomorrow AM.
text:
Ravi Menon: dropping the WIP ADR/design link here for review (enforcement points + policy eval contract). would love eyes esp on trade-offs + auditability: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/hot-route-capacity-protection-adr-wip

Ravi Menon: key question: where do we enforce tier-aware protection? API gateway vs routing service vs runtime scheduler (or layered). constraints: low latency overhead, deterministic/explainable decisions for enterprise escalations, no new cascading failure mode.

Ethan Park: from control plane view, I’m leaning layered:
- gateway: static-ish rate limits/quotas + consistent 429 schema
- routing: admission control when we have fleet pressure signals (queue depth / gpu saturation)
- runtime: last-ditch backpressure to avoid total collapse
biggest risk is adding per-request policy network hop.

Vikram Singh: +1 on layered, but we need to be super clear what executes on the hot path. if gateway calls policy-engine over RPC per request, we’re dead.
what’s the expected overhead target? i’d say <= ~1ms p50 added, and importantly no p99 blowups under pressure.

Jordan Lee: policy eval plan in the draft is “local eval w/ cached policy snapshot”, not synchronous RPC.
- control plane publishes policy bundles (signed) to gateway + routing
- deterministic evaluation in-process
- decision log emitted async (sampling configurable)

auditability: we can guarantee “same inputs => same output” via versioned bundles + stable rules.

Amara Diallo: from gateway side: yes please keep it in-process. we can load the bundle into the existing limiter module.
also we need consistent 429 reasons:
- quota exceeded (window)
- rate limit exceeded (burst)
- overload shedding (admission)
- upstream backpressure (runtime)
if we mix 429/503 without clear semantics, support is going to hate us.

Allison Grant: on metrics/tags: do NOT tag by customer_id in the primary counters. we already got burned w cardinality.
route_group + tier + decision_reason + region should be enough for dashboards.
for audit, log the customer/tenant details in a separate sampled structured log stream (or on-demand query) rather than in metrics.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: tier attribution can’t come from client headers. needs to be derived from auth context (API key -> tenant -> contract tier) and then passed through as signed internal context.
if routing is doing admission based on tier, make sure it can’t be spoofed.

Peter Holtz: concern w “routing does admission”: when routing is degraded (or policy bundle stale), do we fail open or fail closed?
for enterprise SLO protection, i’d rather fail closed for best-effort and fail open for enterprise (i.e., preserve enterprise capacity even if policy eval is busted).
need explicit fallback behavior in ADR.

Mina Haddad: routing angle:
- we can gate best-effort first once queue depth crosses threshold
- we can also redirect best-effort to alternate pools/regions (when allowed)
- enterprise routes: prefer strict admission vs silent fallback unless product signs off (quality changes)
question: do we allow model fallback for /v1/chat/completions enterprise? or only for embeddings/rerank?

Rafael Mendes: SRE asks:
1) how do we debug “why was I 429’d” on-call? need a clear reason code + linkable decision context.
2) if admission control flips on due to pressure, we need an event + annotation on dashboards.
3) runbook should say: check queue depth, shed rate by tier, and which component is shedding (gateway vs routing vs runtime).

Sofia Ivanova: runtime perspective: runtime backpressure is necessary but we should avoid making it the primary enforcement. by the time runtime sees it, latency is already bad.
we can export scheduler_lag + queue_depth and also expose a “reject new work” signal upstream, but that needs to be coarse to avoid thrash.

Vikram Singh: +1. runtime shedding should be the circuit breaker, not the policy engine.
also: please define “capacity signal contract” (names, update cadence, smoothing). if routing is reading a noisy signal, we’ll oscillate and create weird tail latency.

Jordan Lee: auditability details:
- every decision includes: policy_bundle_version, route_group_id, tier, decision (allow/throttle/shed), reason_code
- include a “decision_id” hash over inputs so support can reference it
- store full input tuple in sampled logs only (request_id, tenant_id, route, model, limits)

Amara Diallo: for headers in 429, proposal:
- `x-redwood-retry-after-ms`
- `x-redwood-limit-reason` (enum)
- `x-redwood-policy-version`
body includes same fields + human copy.
need to confirm if we can return retry-after for overload shedding (sometimes answer is “don’t retry immediately”).

Allison Grant: pls avoid per-request “retry-after” that changes wildly; we should bucket/smooth it. otherwise clients retry in lockstep.
maybe return `retry-after` only for quota/rate limits; for overload return a fixed backoff suggestion.

Peter Holtz: on fail open/closed: i want crisp:
- if policy bundle missing/invalid: gateway defaults to existing global limits (safe), routing defaults to “protect enterprise” (shed best-effort earlier)
- if capacity signals missing: routing should degrade to static per-tier budgets, no fancy shaping.

Benji Okafor: rollout/ops notes:
- feature flag at gateway + routing separately
- allowlist enterprise tenants first
- we need rollback switch that *immediately* disables routing admission (since that’s most likely to surprise customers)
- policy bundle version should be pinned per region so we can roll forward/back.

Mina Haddad: question on latency overhead: if gateway does quota + rate check + policy eval, what’s the per-request CPU cost? we should benchmark.
Vikram can we get an agreed budget? (i’ll add to design acceptance criteria)

Vikram Singh: budget suggestion:
- added gateway overhead: p50 < 0.5ms, p99 < 2ms
- routing admission check: should be constant-time and in-process (no extra store read per request)
anything requiring redis roundtrips is out.

Ravi Menon: this is super helpful. i’ll update ADR with:
- layered enforcement (gateway quotas/rate + routing admission + runtime circuit breaker)
- explicit fail open/closed behavior (protect enterprise by default)
- audit log vs metrics cardinality split
- latency budgets + “no synchronous RPC” rule

Rafael Mendes: can we also add an internal debug endpoint spec somewhere? on-call will need a way to ask “given tenant X + route Y, what would policy do?” without tailing logs for an hour.

Jordan Lee: we have a draft for a restricted “policy explain” endpoint (admin-only). will add to ADR as follow-up item; needs authz + audit trail.

Amara Diallo: +1. support asks for “explain 429” all the time. even a console-only tool would help.

Allison Grant: make sure any explain endpoint is rate limited + audited; we don’t want it to become another hot path.

Ravi Menon: action items i’m capturing:
- (Ravi) update ADR w the above + post in #architecture for final sign-off
- (Jordan) add concrete audit fields + sampling guidance
- (Amara) propose final 429 reason codes + headers
- (Allison/Rafael) clarify metrics vs logs + dashboard annotations

Ravi Menon: will repost revised draft tomorrow AM.

title: #architecture: 1759953233
preview:
Rafael Mendes: pulling in #architecture folks for a decision we keep circling. where does the source-of-truth for the public status page component registry live? (component ids, display names, owner, mapping rules, rollout flags). options: repo config vs Confluence page.

Christine Wong: +1. current pain is drift: confluence has “proposed” list, incident-bot has a partial copy, dashboards use slightly diff names.

Irene Choi: repo wins by default for “source of truth” if it’s consumed by tooling. confluence should be a rendered view, not authoritative.

Allison Grant: agree in principle. from observability side: we need the registry to drive dashboard generation + alert labels. we can validate in CI if it’s in git.

Miles Thornton: if it’s in Confluence, the only “validation” is social. also harder to roll back a bad change mid-incident.

Rafael Mendes: concerns I’ve heard:
- oncall needs an easy way to see/edit component list
- product/docs want a human-readable canonical description
- security/legal wants review for anything that changes customer-facing language

Ethan Park: oncall editing in Confluence sounds nice until it breaks automation. we should not be editing customer-facing components during an incident anyway (other than status updates). component taxonomy changes should be planned.

Dev Patel: from Support POV, the main thing is: if the public labels change, support macros and customer comms need to match. having it in git is fine as long as there’s an approval step / heads up.

Logan Wright: if we put it in repo, we can generate the confluence page from it (or at least link it). but the source should be machine-readable.

Christine Wong: what repo? incident-bot repo? observability-pack? main redwood mono?

Allison Grant: prefer a neutral/shared place. observability-pack already has dashboard config + the schema work in flight. also ties to the “signals driving status” standard.

Devon Price: incident-bot just consumes it. i’d rather not host the registry there.

Mina Haddad: needs to be accessible to routing/region taxonomy too. if it’s in observability-pack, is that okay for runtime/platform folks?

Irene Choi: yes if we do proper CODEOWNERS and reviews. we already have cross-team config in repos.

Rafael Mendes: proposal:
1) source-of-truth registry in git (observability-pack) as `statuspage/component-registry.json`
2) JSON schema + CI validation (ids stable, required fields present, no dup display names per region group, etc)
3) Confluence page becomes “published view” that mirrors the registry + additional narrative, but explicitly NOT canonical

Miles Thornton: +1 and add: keep a changelog section in the file header or a `CHANGELOG.md` so we can answer “when did this component split happen?”

Elliot Price: docs angle: if the definitions are in JSON, we can still have docs that explain “what does Hosted API – US/East mean” and link to the registry. but i don’t want docs to be blocked by code review every time we tweak wording.

Michael Grant: legal concern is not the location, it’s control. if a change modifies customer-facing language (display names, descriptions), we need an approval gate. otherwise we risk inconsistent promises.

Naomi Feldman: security/compliance: +1 to auditability. git gives an audit trail. Confluence edits are harder to lock down and review consistently.

Christine Wong: so maybe separate “immutable ids + owner + mapping” vs “long-form description”? the description field could be optional / lightweight.

Allison Grant: i’d still keep a short description in registry for UI/tooling, but not a marketing blurb. anything more verbose lives in docs/confluence.

Dev Patel: review requirement suggestion:
- any change that adds/removes a component OR changes public display_name requires SRE + Support signoff
- changes to ownership/contact can be lighter weight

Irene Choi: also need infra/platform signoff when mapping rules change (service->component). it can impact paging + dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: ok, let’s be concrete on review rules.
Draft:
- CODEOWNERS for `/statuspage/component-registry.json` includes `@eng-sre` + `@eng-platform` + `@observability`
- if `public_display_name` or `public_grouping` changes: require `@support` reviewer + ping `#support`
- if `mapping_rules` changes: require `@eng-platform` reviewer (and `@incident-bot` as FYI)
- if `description` changes only: regular CODEOWNERS ok, but keep it factual

Miles Thornton: add a “rollout flag” in registry (e.g., `enabled: false` until dashboards + incident-bot are ready). prevents partial launch.

Devon Price: +1. incident-bot can read only enabled components.

Logan Wright: CI checks could include “no enabled component without dashboard link” to avoid half-wired entries.

Christine Wong: how do we handle Confluence? i can update the existing taxonomy page to say “generated from registry” and link to the file + ADR.

Elliot Price: i can work with Wei to adjust wording in templates to refer to component ids consistently. but please don’t make Confluence the place people go to copy/paste names.

Naomi Feldman: request: document who can approve emergency changes and how to revert. i’d rather we never do it, but reality.

Rafael Mendes: emergency changes: treat as normal PR with expedited reviewers. no direct pushes. if Sev0 and we absolutely must correct a mislabel, IC can request fast-track review from oncall SRE + one of (Infra/Obs). but ideally we only change status updates, not taxonomy.

Michael Grant: that’s acceptable. add note: any change that alters customer-facing wording during an active incident needs comms lead awareness.

Allison Grant: we should capture all of this in an ADR so it’s not “Slack law.”

Rafael Mendes: yep, i’ll write ADR-014 today with:
- decision: source-of-truth in git (observability-pack)
- confluence is informational mirror
- review requirements + CODEOWNERS
- rollout + validation strategy

Christine Wong: can we also explicitly state: component IDs never change once enabled? only add new + deprecate old.

Irene Choi: +1. stable ids are key for metrics continuity.

Dev Patel: and deprecation should have a window (e.g., 30 days) so support can update macros.

Miles Thornton: (reactions: :thumbsup: x4)

Rafael Mendes: sounds like consensus: repo as source-of-truth, Confluence as mirror. i’ll post ADR draft in this thread for final comments.

Allison Grant: works. once ADR lands, we can unblock the schema PR + mapping work.

Devon Price: +1 please tag me on ADR. i’ll align incident-bot consumption to new path.

Christine Wong: i’ll update the taxonomy page with a banner once ADR is approved.

Rafael Mendes: closing the loop: target to finalize ADR by EOD tomorrow so ENG-4821 can proceed without ambiguity.

title: #architecture: 1753332100
preview:
Karthik Rao: quick design check-in on rollout metadata schema v2. idea: keep runtime wire payload compact, add an optional signed enrichment blob for private installs and for rich clients. want to discuss backwards compatibility, tie-break rules for overlapping pins, and telemetry probe shape.

Zoe Park: love the signed enrichment idea for Private. quick Q: do we need both expires_at ISO and ttl_seconds? i'd prefer single canonical field to avoid conversions in SDKs.

Karthik Rao: agree we should pick one. proposal: canonical on-wire uses ttl_seconds (int) to keep JSON tiny; control plane can surface expires_at in console/ops UI. SDKs can compute expiration if they want human-readable.

Ben Cho: careful: older SDKs may not read ttl_seconds if field name changes. we should include a tiny compatibility hint: "sv" (schema version) integer. older SDKs ignoring unknown fields will be fine; new SDKs use sv to validate.

Mateo Alvarez: tie-breakers: when two pins overlap, deterministic resolver should be: tenant_override > priority_score > earliest_created_ts > lexical_origin_id. compute priority_score server-side. do not put priority on-wire except optional debug.

Karthik Rao: agreed. we'll keep priority server-computed. but include optional debug fields: origin_id and dbg_priority (int). flagged optional so normal clients skip.

Zoe Park: about enrichment signing: propose we add an optional field "enrichment_sig" with base64(sig) and "enrichment_payload" base64(json) so host-side runtime can verify integrity. make the signing key rotation-friendly by including key_id header.

Ben Cho: sounds good. for hosted we don't require signature but we do version the enrichment so SDKs can tolerate missing enrichment. also note: for Private we must document offline key provisioning flow.

Mateo Alvarez: telemetry: add a lightweight probe called rollout_probe: {last_seen_pin, probe_ts_unix, sdk_semver}. keep it sampled (1%) and tolerant of missing fields. this helps measure adoption and stale pins.

Karthik Rao: nice. on collision resolution, we should expose a deterministic resolver endpoint for debugging: /v1/rollouts/resolve?tenant=... returns the chosen pin + resolution trace (list of short tokens describing reasons). for privacy, trace tokens are short codes, not full texts.

Zoe Park: one more: consider binary compacting for devices with bandwidth constraints (e.g., edge runtimes). we could offer an alternative compact encoding (msgpack/base64) but only behind an accept-encoding header. default remains JSON.

Ben Cho: operational concern: TTL semantics. if sdk ignores ttl_seconds, server must enforce a grace window (default 48h) after ttl expiry but mark pins expired in telemetry. tenants can configure grace. must avoid accidental long-lived pins.

Mateo Alvarez: migration plan idea: rollout schema v2 behind a feature flag. step 1: control plane emits sv=2 alongside sv=1 payloads for 2 weeks; step 2: SDK opt-in parse v2 fields; step 3: default switch. sample telemetry to check >95% SDKs can parse.

deploy-bot: rollout-schema-v2 integration tests: 62/64 cases passed. failures: legacy parser test, signed-enrichment missing-key handling.

Karthik Rao: action list: (a) finalize wire fields: sv, model, variant, pin_mode, ttl_seconds, optional: origin_id, dbg_priority, enrichment_payload, enrichment_sig, probe_sample; (b) write Private signing spec + key rotation + provisioning; (c) implement server resolver-trace endpoint; (d) add grace-window config and default to 48h; (e) telemetry probe sampling plan; (f) doc migration plan and SDK changes.

Zoe Park: I'll draft the signing spec and the provisioning playbook.

Ben Cho: I'll add grace-window server defaults and the resolver endpoint sketch.

Mateo Alvarez: I'll wire up the telemetry probe proto and the migration gating checks.

Karthik Rao: i'll assemble the PR for the wire schema + examples and tag you all. thanks.

title: #architecture: 1749014421
preview:
Victor Alvarez: Kicking off arch review thread re: VPC endpoints defaults + DNS behavior for the alpha Terraform modules. Main open question: do we enable private DNS on interface endpoints by default, and how does installer validate prerequisites?

Irene Choi: +1. We need something that “just works” for typical enterprise private subnets, but doesn’t silently break when customer has custom DNS / private hosted zones.

Nina Petrova: From the Private installer side: the top failure mode in pilots is ECR pull failing (no endpoint + no NAT) and then people assume it’s “Redwood broken”. We should bias defaults toward endpoints ON.

Marcus Lin: Security angle: prefer endpoints over NAT egress, esp for regulated. But if we do endpoints, we need to be careful about endpoint policies + SG rules so we’re not punching wide holes.

Victor Alvarez: Current proposal (module defaults):
- S3: Gateway endpoint attached to private route tables
- ECR API + ECR DKR: Interface endpoints
- STS: Interface endpoint
- CloudWatch Logs: Interface endpoint
- KMS: Interface endpoint (optional?)
- EC2 + Autoscaling + ELB: optional / depends on if they run EKS private endpoint + node lifecycle needs
- SSM: optional (nice-to-have for ops)

Enable `private_dns_enabled=true` on interface endpoints by default.

Hanae Suzuki: DNS gotchas: enabling private DNS for ECR endpoints can conflict if customer has a private hosted zone for `ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com` (seen in some locked-down shops). But I think default ON is still correct, just document override.

Simran Kaur: In reference architecture doc we say “no public egress required for image pulls” if endpoints exist. That implicitly assumes private DNS is enabled, otherwise resolving `*.dkr.ecr...` still goes public, right?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. Without private DNS, clients need to use the endpoint-specific DNS names (the long `vpce-xxxx` names). Nobody does that for ECR.

Sean Gallagher: Operationally: installer should fail fast if it can’t pull images / reach control plane deps. Don’t wait until half the cluster is up.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. We should bake preflight checks into installer (or separate “network verify” command). Like: “from inside subnet, can you resolve and connect to ECR, STS, S3?”.

Nina Petrova: Small nuance: installer itself runs wherever customer runs it (often a CI runner or a laptop via VPN). So checks that rely on being inside private subnet can be misleading.

Bruno Silva: We can do AWS API checks too though. Like describe endpoints in VPC and validate they exist + private DNS enabled. That doesn’t prove DNS resolution inside VPC, but catches 80%.

Irene Choi: Can we do both?
1) AWS API validation (DescribeVpcEndpoints + attributes)
2) If installer is running on an instance in the VPC (common in enterprise), do runtime DNS/connectivity checks.

Victor Alvarez: That’s what I was thinking.

Marcus Lin: On “private DNS enabled by default”: +1, but we should also validate VPC flags: `enableDnsSupport` and `enableDnsHostnames`. If those are off, private DNS won’t work even if endpoints exist.

Victor Alvarez: Good call. Terraform module can set those on the VPC resource, but if customer is using an existing VPC we need to either enforce or document.

Kira Thompson: Docs request: spell out “if you bring-your-own VPC, you must have enableDnsSupport/Hostnames = true” and “if you have custom DHCP options / on-prem DNS, check resolver rules.” This comes up every discovery.

Simran Kaur: Also EKS private endpoint mode: for private cluster, you still need certain AWS APIs reachable from nodes (ECR/STS/EC2). If we don’t include EC2 endpoint, node bootstrap can break depending on how hard they lock egress.

Nina Petrova: For Redwood Private, we assume EKS (at least in alpha reference arch). Nodes definitely call EC2 APIs during bootstrapping + CNI. If no NAT, you need more endpoints than just ECR.

Victor Alvarez: So maybe the “required” endpoint set for alpha should be bigger:
- ecr.api
- ecr.dkr
- s3 (gateway)
- sts
- logs
- ec2
And “recommended”:
- kms (if KMS-encrypted stuff)
- autoscaling (if managed nodegroups)
- elasticloadbalancing (if using NLB/ALB)
- monitoring (CloudWatch metrics)
- ssm + ssmmessages + ec2messages (for SSM)

Marcus Lin: Careful with “required”: if they allow NAT egress, they can skip endpoints. But for “no public egress” posture, yes, that’s the required set.

Sean Gallagher: Can we encode that as installer output?
- Detect NAT route to IGW? (well, NAT implies route to NAT in private RT)
- If no NAT present, then enforce endpoints set
- If NAT present, warn if endpoints missing but don’t fail

Bruno Silva: NAT detection via AWS APIs is possible (describe route tables for `0.0.0.0/0 -> nat-...`). Not perfect but good.

Rafael Mendes: Another check: verify endpoints are in the same subnets as the cluster nodes (or at least reachable). We’ve seen endpoints created only in one AZ/subnet and then cross-AZ traffic gets weird / costs.

Victor Alvarez: Module default will create interface endpoints in all private subnets (one per AZ). But yes, installer can verify `SubnetIds` covers expected.

Hanae Suzuki: Endpoint SG rules: we should keep it simple. The endpoint ENIs should allow 443 inbound from node SG (or from private subnet CIDRs). Least privilege is nice, but if we get it wrong we strand people.

Marcus Lin: Least privilege suggestion: allow 443 from “cluster nodes SG” + “installer/bastion SG” if provided. Avoid CIDR-wide if we can. But we need an escape hatch.

Irene Choi: For alpha, I’d rather have a working default than ultra-tight SG logic that’s fragile. But we can still do SG-to-SG.

Victor Alvarez: Proposed endpoint SG default:
- Ingress: tcp/443 from `var.allowed_client_security_group_ids` (default: node SG, and optionally a shared “private clients” SG)
- Egress: allow all (AWS endpoints are managed anyway)

Kira Thompson: How do we explain private DNS risk to customers? We should include a doc note:
- If you already manage private hosted zones for `amazonaws.com` names, set `private_dns_enabled=false` and configure Route53 resolver rules accordingly.

Simran Kaur: + link to AWS docs on endpoint private DNS + split-horizon. (People mix it up with “private hosted zone”.)

Nina Petrova: Installer-side: please add explicit error strings. Something like:
"Missing VPC endpoint for ECR (com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr). If your private subnets have no NAT egress, image pulls will fail. Fix: enable VPC endpoint + private DNS or add NAT/proxy."

Sean Gallagher: Can we include a one-liner command in the error that works from inside cluster?
`nslookup <acct>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`
and
`curl -I https://api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`

Bruno Silva: We can put that into troubleshooting section. Preflight can also run a basic DNS resolution check if it’s inside VPC.

Victor Alvarez: Rough installer preflight pseudo-output:
```
[network] vpc_id=vpc-1234
[network] dns_support=true dns_hostnames=true
[network] nat_detected=false
[network] checking required endpoints (no NAT detected)
  - ecr.api: OK (vpce-aaa, private_dns=true, subnets=3)
  - ecr.dkr: FAIL (not found)
  - sts: OK (vpce-bbb, private_dns=true)
  - logs: OK (vpce-ccc, private_dns=true)
  - s3 gateway: OK (vpce-ddd, rt_associations=3)
Result: FAIL. Missing endpoints: ecr.dkr
```

Marcus Lin: Looks good. Also check endpoint policy is not overly restrictive? Hard to validate generically. Maybe just warn if policy is non-default and we see explicit Deny?

Irene Choi: That might be overreach for alpha. But we can at least surface “endpoint policy present” in debug output.

Rafael Mendes: One more DNS item: if they use on-prem DNS via inbound resolver endpoints, and they forward `amazonaws.com` zones, they can break private DNS. Installer should tell them: “check Route53 resolver rules / DHCP options domain-name-servers.”

Hanae Suzuki: +1. We can’t introspect that fully, but we can add a targeted warning when private DNS is enabled and VPC has custom DHCP options set (domain-name-servers not AmazonProvidedDNS).

Victor Alvarez: Nice. That’s detectable.

Nina Petrova: Action items from this thread:
1) Module defaults: endpoints ON, private DNS ON for interface endpoints, S3 gateway for S3
2) Document override for private DNS + hosted zone conflicts
3) Installer preflight: describe endpoints + privateDnsEnabled + VPC DNS flags + NAT detection; fail if missing required endpoints when no NAT
4) Troubleshooting text for DNS resolution + on-prem resolver forwarding pitfalls

Irene Choi: Works for me. Victor can you capture in ADR comment + ping Bruno (module) + Nina (installer) so we’re aligned?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. I’ll summarize + add to the PR notes. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: + please make sure preflight runs before any expensive provisioning steps. We want fast feedback.

Victor Alvarez: Noted. Closing this out unless more concerns.

Victor Alvarez: Kicking off arch review thread re: VPC endpoints defaults + DNS behavior for the alpha Terraform modules. Main open question: do we enable private DNS on interface endpoints by default, and how does installer validate prerequisites?

Irene Choi: +1. We need something that “just works” for typical enterprise private subnets, but doesn’t silently break when customer has custom DNS / private hosted zones.

Nina Petrova: From the Private installer side: the top failure mode in pilots is ECR pull failing (no endpoint + no NAT) and then people assume it’s “Redwood broken”. We should bias defaults toward endpoints ON.

Marcus Lin: Security angle: prefer endpoints over NAT egress, esp for regulated. But if we do endpoints, we need to be careful about endpoint policies + SG rules so we’re not punching wide holes.

Victor Alvarez: Current proposal (module defaults):
- S3: Gateway endpoint attached to private route tables
- ECR API + ECR DKR: Interface endpoints
- STS: Interface endpoint
- CloudWatch Logs: Interface endpoint
- KMS: Interface endpoint (optional?)
- EC2 + Autoscaling + ELB: optional / depends on if they run EKS private endpoint + node lifecycle needs
- SSM: optional (nice-to-have for ops)

Enable `private_dns_enabled=true` on interface endpoints by default.

Hanae Suzuki: DNS gotchas: enabling private DNS for ECR endpoints can conflict if customer has a private hosted zone for `ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com` (seen in some locked-down shops). But I think default ON is still correct, just document override.

Simran Kaur: In reference architecture doc we say “no public egress required for image pulls” if endpoints exist. That implicitly assumes private DNS is enabled, otherwise resolving `*.dkr.ecr...` still goes public, right?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. Without private DNS, clients need to use the endpoint-specific DNS names (the long `vpce-xxxx` names). Nobody does that for ECR.

Nina Petrova: Small nuance: installer itself runs wherever customer runs it (often a CI runner or a laptop via VPN). So checks that rely on being inside private subnet can be misleading.

Bruno Silva: We can do AWS API checks too though. Like describe endpoints in VPC and validate they exist + private DNS enabled. That doesn’t prove DNS resolution inside VPC, but catches 80%.

Irene Choi: Can we do both?

- s3 (gateway)
- sts

- ec2

- autoscaling (if managed nodegroups)
- elasticloadbalancing (if using NLB/ALB)
- monitoring (CloudWatch metrics)
- ssm + ssmmessages + ec2messages (for SSM)

title: #architecture: 1743429088
preview:
Quentin Harris: Kicking off schema design thread for model metadata v1. Main open questions:
1) source of truth: model registry vs control plane DB?
2) public vs internal fields separation (and how hard we enforce)
3) backwards compat / semver semantics for model “versions” vs “variants”
4) deprecation semantics (announce vs removal vs EOL) esp for Private bundles

Ethan Park: +1. Control plane is currently the “truth” for serving config (routing, availability), registry is the “truth” for catalog (model family, tokenizer, quant profiles). If we pick two truths we’re gonna keep repeating this project.

Michael Nguyen: Agree. Also reminder: migrations are already scary. If we decide registry is truth, control plane should be read-through + cache and only store serving-state (promotions/rollout state) and maybe some denormalized fields.

Dylan Brooks: From Console POV: we need a single API that says “this model version changed” + a stable record to diff. If the diff pulls from 3 places it will be impossible to generate consistent release notes.

Jada Williams: Question: can we define the schema as “registry-owned record” and then have control plane append a separate “deployment availability + rollout eligibility” sub-record keyed by deployment? Otherwise we’re mixing static metadata (tokenizer, compat) with dynamic state (rollout phase, region enablement).

Quentin Harris: That’s the tension. Schema doc I drafted assumes a single record with sections:
- identity (model_id, variant_id, quant_profile)
- version (semver-ish)
- compatibility (tokenizer hash, runtime min version, supported features)
- notes (public/internal)
- lifecycle (status: active/deprecated/removed + dates)
- availability (hosted/dedicated/private flags + region list)
But availability feels like control plane truth.

Diego Santos: Multi-deployment consistency: availability differs by mode (Hosted can be “on” earlier; Private later due to bundle cadence). If registry is global truth, we need per-deployment timelines + “planned” states or we’ll publish notes that look like we shipped in Private when we didn’t.

Naomi Feldman: On public/internal: we need hard enforcement that internal never leaks. I’d prefer explicit fields rather than “same field but redaction rules”. Like `public_summary` vs `internal_summary`, `public_risks` vs `internal_risks`.

Marcus Lin: +1. Compliance angle: we can’t rely on UI filtering alone. We need data-model-level separation + validation in CI.

Sahana Iyer: CI validation idea: JSON Schema enforces that anything under `public.*` is safe. Also enforce that `internal.*` fields are never referenced by the changelog generator’s public template path.

Connor O'Brien: Release eng take: don’t overcomplicate the pipeline. If we have “public/internal” as parallel subtrees, the generator can do:
- public notes feed = only `public.*` plus safe derived fields
- internal feed = union of both
Then we can add a CI check that fails if `public.*` is empty for a promotion event (unless event type is internal-only).

Logan Wright: For eventing: if we want auditability and release notes, we should separate:
- “metadata changed” event (includes schema_hash + diff pointers)
- “version promoted” event (this is the customer-visible moment)
Release notes should attach to promotion, not every edit.

Ethan Park: +1000. Also source of truth: I’m leaning registry is truth for the “static” record. Control plane is truth for “state”, but state should be computed off registry record IDs (not duplicated).

Michael Nguyen: If control plane stores availability/rollout in its DB, then release notes generation must join registry + control plane state at promotion time. That’s fine if we treat promotion as a control plane action.

Quentin Harris: So… propose split:
A) Registry owns ModelMetadata (immutable-ish except new version records)
B) Control plane owns DeploymentState (regions, rollouts, eligibility)
C) Changelog generator consumes events emitted by control plane at promotion time; event references registry record(s) via IDs + schema_hash

Jada Williams: That works as long as the “public vs internal” fields live in registry. Otherwise we’re going to have “internal reasons” in control plane state that leak via events.

Naomi Feldman: Careful: “why promoted/rolled back” is usually internal. Support wants it, customers don’t. We should ensure event has `reason_internal` separate from `reason_public` and generator only uses the public one.

Marcus Lin: Audit logging wants the internal reason though. But audit logs are not the same as customer-visible release notes. We can emit two events:
- audit event (internal, full payload, retained)
- release-note event (public-safe, minimal)
Or one event with strict classification labels.

Olga Petrov: Prefer one event schema with classification per field (e.g. `visibility=public|internal`). But we need to make sure downstream consumers enforce it consistently.

Sahana Iyer: One event with classification is tricky in JSON Schema because it becomes nested/complex fast. Two events is simpler to validate.

Dylan Brooks: Console can handle two feeds. We already have separate RBAC surfaces: customer-facing release notes vs internal admin/audit view. So splitting seems fine.

Diego Santos: Re backwards compatibility: how are we defining “breaking”? For customers pinned to `model@1.2.3`, we should never swap weights/tokenizer under the same version. So version must be content-addressed or we guarantee immutability.

Quentin Harris: Agree. Rule should be:
- `model_version` is immutable once promoted to any environment.
- any change to weights/tokenizer/runtime requirements => new version.
- patch/minor/major semantics: major = behavior/compat change (tokenizer change, output format changes, tool calling schema changes), minor = perf/quality changes within compat envelope, patch = bugfixes/infra-only changes that don’t affect outputs? (though output can still drift with kernel changes).

Jordan Lee: Rollout policy engine needs a machine-readable “risk” and “compat” signal. If we only have human-written notes, we can’t gate rollouts.

Ethan Park: +1. We need fields like:
- `compat.tokenizer_id` + `compat.tokenizer_rev`
- `compat.api_features` (supports_json_mode, supports_tool_calling, etc)
- `rollout.risk_tier` (low/med/high)
- `rollout.eligible_for_auto_promotion` boolean

Jada Williams: But make sure those are public? Risk tier might be ok to publish as “Low/Medium/High risk” but internal criteria shouldn’t show.

Naomi Feldman: Public can have “risk: low/medium/high” + generic explanation. Internal can include “triggered by kernel swap, new kv cache manager, etc”.

Connor O'Brien: Deprecations: we need a standard timeline. Suggest:
- status: active -> deprecated (with `deprecated_at`, `removal_earliest_at`)
- removal: removed (no longer served in Hosted)
- for Private: “supported until bundle N” or “supported through date X”
We should avoid promising exact dates in public notes unless we’re sure.

Olga Petrov: Enterprise asks for minimum 90-day deprecation notice. But reality: security issues can shorten that. We need semantics for “emergency deprecation”.

Marcus Lin: If emergency, we can set `deprecation.reason = security` and allow shorter windows. But again: careful what’s public. “Security update” yes, but not details.

Michael Nguyen: Also: backwards compat constraints for Private bundles. Private customers can be 2-3 versions behind. We must keep metadata for removed models available for audit + offline release notes even if Hosted no longer serves them.

Diego Santos: +1. Registry should retain historical versions forever (or at least very long). Removal means “not available” not “deleted”.

Sahana Iyer: Validation rule we should add: `lifecycle.removed_at` cannot be set without `lifecycle.deprecated_at` unless `deprecation.emergency=true`.

Quentin Harris: Summarizing emerging consensus:
- Registry owns immutable per-version metadata records (including public/internal notes)
- Control plane owns deployment/region/rollout state and emits promotion events referencing registry record IDs
- Two event streams: audit (internal full), release-note (public-safe minimal)
- Versions immutable; semver-ish with clear rules; compatibility + risk fields are machine-readable
- Deprecation semantics: active/deprecated/removed with dates; removal != delete; Private gets additional “bundle mapping” fields

Dylan Brooks: Works for Console. Next: we need to decide exact shape of `public` vs `internal` in schema. I’m in favor of explicit trees: `notes_public`, `notes_internal`.

Naomi Feldman: Please avoid “freeform markdown everywhere”. We’ll need structured fields for templates (summary, impact, action_required, rollback_guidance).

Connor O'Brien: +1. Release notes generator needs stable slots. Suggest public template fields:
- `summary`
- `customer_impact`
- `action_required` (optional)
- `rollback` (optional)
- `references` (docs links)
And internal adds:
- `change_owner`
- `reason_internal`
- `incident_links`
- `eval_run_ids`

Jordan Lee: Also include `compat.replaces` / `fallbacks_to` list for automated fallback policies.

Ethan Park: Ok. Quentin can you capture this in ADR language? Specifically “source of truth and publishing workflow”.

Quentin Harris: Yep, I’ll write up and tag @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Naomi Feldman for review.

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: and please include explicit statement: “No internal-only fields may be present in public artifacts, including docs build outputs.”

Sahana Iyer: I’ll draft the CI checks list for schema validation + redaction tests. If we do two event types, I can add a fixture test ensuring public templates reject internal fields.

Logan Wright: If you need a canonical field list for event classification, I can provide from analytics platform side. Also recommend adding idempotency key: `event_id = hash(model_id, version, action, ts)`.

Michael Nguyen: One more: for “source of truth”, can we state explicitly: control plane can *not* mutate registry metadata, only reference it. Only registry PR flow can add new versions / change metadata.

Ethan Park: Yes please. That’s the only way we avoid a write-write split brain.

Quentin Harris: Cool. I’ll incorporate. Thanks all.

Sahana Iyer: One event with classification is tricky in JSON Schema because it becomes nested/complex fast. Two events is simpler to validate.

Dylan Brooks: Console can handle two feeds. We already have separate RBAC surfaces: customer-facing release notes vs internal admin/audit view. So splitting seems fine.

Diego Santos: Re backwards compatibility: how are we defining “breaking”? For customers pinned to `model@1.2.3`, we should never swap weights/tokenizer under the same version. So version must be content-addressed or we guarantee immutability.

Quentin Harris: Agree. Rule should be:
- `model_version` is immutable once promoted to any environment.
- any change to weights/tokenizer/runtime requirements => new version.
- patch/minor/major semantics: major = behavior/compat change (tokenizer change, output format changes, tool calling schema changes), minor = perf/quality changes within compat envelope, patch = bugfixes/infra-only changes that don’t affect outputs? (though output can still drift with kernel changes).

Jordan Lee: Rollout policy engine needs a machine-readable “risk” and “compat” signal. If we only have human-written notes, we can’t gate rollouts.

Ethan Park: +1. We need fields like:
- `compat.tokenizer_id` + `compat.tokenizer_rev`
- `compat.api_features` (supports_json_mode, supports_tool_calling, etc)
- `rollout.risk_tier` (low/med/high)
- `rollout.eligible_for_auto_promotion` boolean

Jada Williams: But make sure those are public? Risk tier might be ok to publish as “Low/Medium/High risk” but internal criteria shouldn’t show.

Naomi Feldman: Public can have “risk: low/medium/high” + generic explanation. Internal can include “triggered by kernel swap, new kv cache manager, etc”.

Connor O'Brien: Deprecations: we need a standard timeline. Suggest:
- status: active -> deprecated (with `deprecated_at`, `removal_earliest_at`)
- removal: removed (no longer served in Hosted)
- for Private: “supported until bundle N” or “supported through date X”
We should avoid promising exact dates in public notes unless we’re sure.

Olga Petrov: Enterprise asks for minimum 90-day deprecation notice. But reality: security issues can shorten that. We need semantics for “emergency deprecation”.

Marcus Lin: If emergency, we can set `deprecation.reason = security` and allow shorter windows. But again: careful what’s public. “Security update” yes, but not details.

Michael Nguyen: Also: backwards compat constraints for Private bundles. Private customers can be 2-3 versions behind. We must keep metadata for removed models available for audit + offline release notes even if Hosted no longer serves them.

Diego Santos: +1. Registry should retain historical versions forever (or at least very long). Removal means “not available” not “deleted”.

Sahana Iyer: Validation rule we should add: `lifecycle.removed_at` cannot be set without `lifecycle.deprecated_at` unless `deprecation.emergency=true`.

Quentin Harris: Summarizing emerging consensus:
- Registry owns immutable per-version metadata records (including public/internal notes)
- Control plane owns deployment/region/rollout state and emits promotion events referencing registry record IDs
- Two event streams: audit (internal full), release-note (public-safe minimal)
- Versions immutable; semver-ish with clear rules; compatibility + risk fields are machine-readable
- Deprecation semantics: active/deprecated/removed with dates; removal != delete; Private gets additional “bundle mapping” fields

Dylan Brooks: Works for Console. Next: we need to decide exact shape of `public` vs `internal` in schema. I’m in favor of explicit trees: `notes_public`, `notes_internal`.

Naomi Feldman: Please avoid “freeform markdown everywhere”. We’ll need structured fields for templates (summary, impact, action_required, rollback_guidance).

Connor O'Brien: +1. Release notes generator needs stable slots. Suggest public template fields:
- `summary`
- `customer_impact`
- `action_required` (optional)
- `rollback` (optional)
- `references` (docs links)
And internal adds:
- `change_owner`
- `reason_internal`
- `incident_links`
- `eval_run_ids`

Jordan Lee: Also include `compat.replaces` / `fallbacks_to` list for automated fallback policies.

Ethan Park: Ok. Quentin can you capture this in ADR language? Specifically “source of truth and publishing workflow”.

Quentin Harris: Yep, I’ll write up and tag @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Naomi Feldman for review.

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: and please include explicit statement: “No internal-only fields may be present in public artifacts, including docs build outputs.”

Sahana Iyer: I’ll draft the CI checks list for schema validation + redaction tests. If we do two event types, I can add a fixture test ensuring public templates reject internal fields.

Logan Wright: If you need a canonical field list for event classification, I can provide from analytics platform side. Also recommend adding idempotency key: `event_id = hash(model_id, version, action, ts)`.

Michael Nguyen: One more: for “source of truth”, can we state explicitly: control plane can *not* mutate registry metadata, only reference it. Only registry PR flow can add new versions / change metadata.

Ethan Park: Yes please. That’s the only way we avoid a write-write split brain.

Quentin Harris: Cool. I’ll incorporate. Thanks all.

title: #architecture: 1741247710
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off ADR review for Runtime LTS branching/versioning. Main question: do we maintain a dedicated `runtime-1.18-lts` branch (with patches), or do tags-only on mainline (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts` pointing at selected commits)? Looking for eyes on tradeoffs: dependency mgmt, long-term maint cost, and customer pinning semantics (Private/Helm).

Noah Patel: Tags-only feels attractive on paper but I’m worried it collapses the “stable baseline” story. If main keeps moving (scheduler/kernels), tags-only means we’re constantly re-validating a new head for every patch.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. LTS promise is “slower-change”. If we cut from main each month, that’s not LTS, that’s just regular release train with a different label.

Sahana Iyer: From CI/build: tags-only is painful for reproducible builds. We need deterministic toolchain + pinned deps + provenance artifacts. If mainline build scripts evolve, an older tag may not be buildable without archaeology. A branch lets us freeze build plumbing for that line.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: if a Sev1 hits on an LTS customer, we want minimal diff from last known-good. With tags-only you’re effectively pulling in unrelated deltas just to get the fix, unless you do some elaborate cherry-pick dance anyway.

Rafael Mendes: Also operationally: release branch lets us run separate canary + SLO dashboards scoped to that line. If it’s just tags on main, it gets ambiguous what “line” a customer is on when we’re debugging.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel side: we already talked about freezing autotune defaults. That’s basically “branch behavior”. Doing it via tags-only means we’re trying to keep incompatible defaults coexisting on main which is a mess.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling: same issue. There are heuristics we explicitly *don’t* want in LTS (experimental batching / load shedding tweaks). Branch gives us a clean “no, not in this line” answer.

Devon Price: The only upside I see for tags-only is fewer branches to babysit + less backport conflict resolution. But… we’re still backporting, just not calling it that.

Quentin Harris: Compatibility matrix / packaging: Private installer and Helm need a stable image tag scheme. It’s much clearer if it’s `runtime:1.18.1-lts` built from `runtime-1.18-lts` branch with a corresponding git tag. Tags-only on main is doable but makes provenance and “what changed since last patch” harder to explain.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Security: branch helps with vulnerability response. We can scope patches to the supported line, document CVEs, and avoid unrelated changes. For audit, “supported branch + signed artifacts + SBOM” is a familiar enterprise pattern.

Talia Benson: Trying to capture: branch = clearer product semantics + stable build/CI config + minimal-diff backports; tags-only = lower overhead but undermines baseline and forces wide diffs over time.

Connor O'Brien: Counterpoint: branch cost. We’ll need to keep dependencies (CUDA toolchain, NCCL, third party libs) pinned and occasionally uplift them for security fixes anyway. Does branch make that worse?

Sahana Iyer: It makes it explicit, not worse. Either way we need patch-level uplift for security. With a branch we can track “toolchain uplift” as an LTS change category and gate it with perf + determinism checks.

Noah Patel: Dependency mgmt detail: runtime has some “generated kernels / codegen outputs” that can drift. If main updates generator, tags-only means backports might require re-running generator with a newer version (non-repro). Branch can freeze generator version and keep outputs stable.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also “micro-architecture tuning tables” change fairly often. We can freeze + document override knobs for LTS, rather than chasing main updates.

Sean Gallagher: Maintenance cost question: how many lines are we expecting concurrently? If it’s 1 active LTS (1.18) and maybe a short overlap during cutover (1.19), that’s manageable. If we promise 3 active LTS lines, different story.

Mateo Alvarez: Plan is single active LTS at a time, 12 months support window, overlap for 4–6 weeks during next LTS branch cut. We should write that in ADR to cap the sprawl.

Rafael Mendes: On-call/escalation: branch lets us attach “mandatory patch” flags to a line. For tags-only, there’s a risk customers pin to an older tag and never take the security update because it doesn’t look like a “patch train”.

Devon Price: Tooling: backport labels + cherry-pick bot assumes a target branch. Tags-only would mean cherry-picking into… what exactly? You’d still need a staging branch or release branch to land the patch before tagging.

Connor O'Brien: Good point: our automation tickets (backport bot, release branch CI) basically assume `runtime-*-lts` exists.

Quentin Harris: Customer semantics: Dedicated/Private want to say “we run runtime-1.18-lts, patch 3” and have that map to artifacts, checksums, notes, and support policy. Branch name shows up in internal dashboards too.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Also aligns with compliance norms: “maintenance branch with security backports” is a thing auditors understand. We can map CVE remediation to a branch and tag.

Noah Patel: So what’s the main argument *against* branch? Mostly backport conflicts and long-term drift. But drift is controlled by policy: only Sev1/Sev2/security, minimal diffs, perf gates.

Sofia Ivanova: Conflicts: we’ll get some, but scheduler has good test coverage now + we’re adding targeted smoke tests on release branches. That should reduce regressions even when conflicts happen.

Sahana Iyer: If we do branch, we need strict rules for merging *from* LTS back to main (or not). I suggest: never merge LTS into main, only cherry-pick from main to LTS. Otherwise we create weird history.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. LTS is downstream only.

Sean Gallagher: Another nuance: perf gate stability. If tags-only, perf gates would have to run on main head for each patch, and you’ll get more variance (different kernels/heuristics). Branch helps keep perf noise down and makes regressions easier to attribute.

Mateo Alvarez: Sounds like consensus: dedicated LTS branch is the right call, with explicit constraints:
- only one active LTS line
- downstream-only cherry-picks
- pinned build/toolchain for reproducibility
- clearly documented dependency uplift policy (security-driven)

Talia Benson: Action items I’m hearing:
1) Connor to update ADR text to explicitly reject tags-only and call out why (baseline stability + build reproducibility + customer pinning).
2) Sahana to add a short section on build determinism and why branch matters.
3) Quentin to review the pinning semantics wording for Private/Helm.

Connor O'Brien: Yup. I’ll push ADR edits today and re-request sign-off. If anyone feels strongly we should keep tags-only as a fallback, speak now.

Devon Price: No objection. Maybe include one line: tags still exist for each patch (`1.18.1-lts`), but they’re cut from the LTS branch.

Connor O'Brien: Good call.

Rafael Mendes: +1 and add “waiver process” reference for perf gates (SRE approval) so people don’t bypass it under pressure.

Connor O'Brien: Noted.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: Let’s land it. We can revisit if branch maintenance cost explodes, but right now branch is the only way to make the promise real.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: 

Dr. Aisha Rahman: :thumbsup: Please make sure ADR mentions security OOB patches can be cut directly on LTS branch with tightened approval.

Connor O'Brien: Will do. Thanks all.

title: #architecture: 1740018450
preview:
Allison Grant: Dropping the ADR draft here for review: ADR-0127 TP watchdog + abort semantics (WIP) https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-0127 — main open questions are (1) how abort propagates to API layer and (2) retry behavior + drain vs attempt recovery.

Noah Patel: thx. tl;dr from runtime side: if we declare a TP step stalled, we should assume rank state is unsafe. I’m leaning “drain replica, fail in-flight requests with explicit retryable error, and let router re-place”. In-process recovery is tempting but we’ve been burned by partially wedged NCCL communicators.

Sean Gallagher: +1 to conservative. From incident side, anything that keeps a wedged replica taking traffic is a nightmare (cascades into queue buildup + p99 spikes). Drain/replace is the only thing that actually resolves the symptom.

Rafael Mendes: can we be super crisp in the ADR about *what* is drained? Like: stop accepting new requests, cancel queued work, then hard restart container vs just remove from pool?

Noah Patel: Good call. I propose:
1) mark replica “unhealthy: tp_stall” in control plane
2) stop scheduling new work to it immediately (load-shed)
3) best-effort abort current batch (return error)
4) force process exit within N seconds (so k8s replaces)

Sofia Ivanova: question: what about the scheduler state? If we abort a batch mid-decode, do we risk leaving shared queues in a bad state (e.g. requests pinned to that replica)? I want to ensure we’re not “leaking” tokens/slots.

Jacob Stein: if the router sees explicit error code and “drain event” we can correlate + avoid retry loops to same replica. We need a stable signal (metric + structured log) that the gateway uses for routing decisions.

Allison Grant: ADR currently says “abort surfaces as 503 with retryable=true”. Does that match gateway semantics today? I think some clients treat 503 as retryable, but not all.

Connor O'Brien: For Hosted: gateway does limited retries on certain upstream codes already, but we avoid retrying streaming. For Dedicated: customer gateway varies; we should document recommended retry behavior and also keep it safe server-side.

Omar El-Sayed: in the PRs we’re planning structured error payloads. propose an explicit `error.code` so customers can key off it rather than HTTP status. Something like `RUNTIME_TP_STALL_ABORTED`.

Hiro Tanaka: We need to be careful w/ “best-effort abort current batch”. If NCCL is stuck in a collective, the abort can’t actually unwind anything. So “return error” might not happen unless we time out at a higher layer and cut the process.

Noah Patel: yep. That’s why “force exit within N seconds” is important. We should avoid waiting on CUDA calls during shutdown. Basically: watchdog thread triggers -> signal main -> if no ack quickly, `_exit(1)`.

Sean Gallagher: for incident response, I’d rather have a clean crash+restart than a zombie that keeps GPUs allocated and never responds. Please make the ADR explicit: prioritize system health over “try to save the request”.

Grace Kim: API propagation detail: for non-streaming requests, we can respond quickly if we detect stall before we enter the blocking region. But if we’re already blocked, the only real response is the client sees timeout / connection reset when the process exits.

Allison Grant: Should ADR specify two cases?
- “preemptive” abort: detected stall risk before entering blocking collective -> return structured error
- “hard” abort: stall confirmed inside blocking -> terminate process, client gets 502/connection drop

Noah Patel: I’d support documenting both, but we should still *try* to standardize on the structured error for anything we can.

Rafael Mendes: retry behavior: we should NOT auto-retry in the runtime. That’s a recipe for duplicate work and more load during an incident. But the router/gateway can retry with caps.

Connor O'Brien: agree. At most, gateway retries once for non-streaming, only if request is idempotent. For streaming, no retry by default.

Paula Mendes: product angle: Dedicated customers are going to ask “will retries cause duplicate outputs / billing?” We should call out that client-initiated retries can lead to multiple completions unless they use idempotency keys.

Omar El-Sayed: Can we add a note: recommend `Idempotency-Key` support for generation endpoints? If not universally supported, at least for Dedicated (or document limitation).

Allison Grant: We have partial support in API gateway, but not consistently across endpoints. ADR can reference “if idempotency is present, gateway may retry once”. Otherwise: surface retryable error but do not retry automatically.

Tomáš Novák: on drain vs recovery: please avoid “recovery in-process” language in the runbook. Operators will interpret it as “wait for it to heal”. We need deterministic steps: drain -> replace.

Sofia Ivanova: Another nuance: if we drain on any watchdog trigger, false positives could cause churn (and reduce capacity). ADR should include default timeout philosophy: start conservative (longer) and tighten with canary.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also: timeouts should be different for prefill vs decode. Prefill on long context can legitimately take longer. If watchdog is per “collective step”, we should base thresholds on observed durations + p99.

Noah Patel: ADR currently proposes:
- decode step watchdog: 2s default
- collective watchdog: 5s default
These might be too aggressive for 70B long context. Maybe do model-size + context-length scaling.

Sean Gallagher: as long as we can feature-flag and roll out per cluster/account. I don’t want a global default change without a canary + clear rollback.

Allison Grant: ok, I’ll update ADR with:
- explicit drain/replace as required action on confirmed stall
- two propagation modes (structured 503 vs connection reset on hard abort)
- retry guidance: no runtime retries; gateway may retry once only for non-streaming + idempotency
- clarify billing/output duplication caveat

Jacob Stein: please also include the exact error payload shape so downstream can key off it. Suggest:
```json
{ "error": { "code": "RUNTIME_TP_STALL_ABORTED", "message": "TP watchdog triggered; replica draining", "retryable": true, "details": { "model": "...", "replica": "...", "rank": "...", "stall_reason": "nccl_collective_timeout" } } }
```

Omar El-Sayed: + add a `request_id` so support can correlate. We already have it in headers.

Connor O'Brien: logging: please ensure abort emits a single drain event (rate-limited) otherwise paging noise. (We’ve seen “1 request = 100 logs” patterns.)

Rafael Mendes: Action items?

Allison Grant: AI summary / next steps:
- Allison: revise ADR w/ drain semantics + propagation modes + retry/idempotency guidance (today)
- Noah + Hiro: propose timeout defaults / scaling rules for canary (by EOW)
- Jacob + Omar: confirm error code + payload fields align w/ gateway + tracing (tomorrow)
- Sean/Rafael/Tomáš: sanity check runbook wording matches ADR (after update)

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please tag me on the updated ADR; I’ll sign off once it’s explicit about “drain/replace, do not wait”.

Noah Patel: same. I’ll comment inline on the timeout section once updated.

Rafael Mendes: retry behavior: we should NOT auto-retry in the runtime. That’s a recipe for duplicate work and more load during an incident. But the router/gateway can retry with caps.

Connor O'Brien: agree. At most, gateway retries once for non-streaming, only if request is idempotent. For streaming, no retry by default.

Paula Mendes: product angle: Dedicated customers are going to ask “will retries cause duplicate outputs / billing?” We should call out that client-initiated retries can lead to multiple completions unless they use idempotency keys.

Omar El-Sayed: Can we add a note: recommend `Idempotency-Key` support for generation endpoints? If not universally supported, at least for Dedicated (or document limitation).

Allison Grant: We have partial support in API gateway, but not consistently across endpoints. ADR can reference “if idempotency is present, gateway may retry once”. Otherwise: surface retryable error but do not retry automatically.

Tomáš Novák: on drain vs recovery: please avoid “recovery in-process” language in the runbook. Operators will interpret it as “wait for it to heal”. We need deterministic steps: drain -> replace.

Sofia Ivanova: Another nuance: if we drain on any watchdog trigger, false positives could cause churn (and reduce capacity). ADR should include default timeout philosophy: start conservative (longer) and tighten with canary.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also: timeouts should be different for prefill vs decode. Prefill on long context can legitimately take longer. If watchdog is per “collective step”, we should base thresholds on observed durations + p99.

Noah Patel: ADR currently proposes:
- decode step watchdog: 2s default
- collective watchdog: 5s default
These might be too aggressive for 70B long context. Maybe do model-size + context-length scaling.

Sean Gallagher: as long as we can feature-flag and roll out per cluster/account. I don’t want a global default change without a canary + clear rollback.

Allison Grant: ok, I’ll update ADR with:
- explicit drain/replace as required action on confirmed stall
- two propagation modes (structured 503 vs connection reset on hard abort)
- retry guidance: no runtime retries; gateway may retry once only for non-streaming + idempotency
- clarify billing/output duplication caveat

Jacob Stein: please also include the exact error payload shape so downstream can key off it. Suggest:
```json
{ "error": { "code": "RUNTIME_TP_STALL_ABORTED", "message": "TP watchdog triggered; replica draining", "retryable": true, "details": { "model": "...", "replica": "...", "rank": "...", "stall_reason": "nccl_collective_timeout" } } }
```

Omar El-Sayed: + add a `request_id` so support can correlate. We already have it in headers.

Connor O'Brien: logging: please ensure abort emits a single drain event (rate-limited) otherwise paging noise. (We’ve seen “1 request = 100 logs” patterns.)

Rafael Mendes: Action items?

Allison Grant: AI summary / next steps:
- Allison: revise ADR w/ drain semantics + propagation modes + retry/idempotency guidance (today)
- Noah + Hiro: propose timeout defaults / scaling rules for canary (by EOW)
- Jacob + Omar: confirm error code + payload fields align w/ gateway + tracing (tomorrow)
- Sean/Rafael/Tomáš: sanity check runbook wording matches ADR (after update)

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please tag me on the updated ADR; I’ll sign off once it’s explicit about “drain/replace, do not wait”.

Noah Patel: same. I’ll comment inline on the timeout section once updated.

title: #architecture: 1739993210
preview:
Asha Rao: Heads up: planning a quick EU residency handoff drill next Thursday. Aim: ensure proxy-driven promote works without any non-EU writes. Anyone see problems?

Ben Carter: +1. Clarify: soft-affinity active/active path or emergency promote (active/passive)? We should test both but focus on the promote flow.

Asha Rao: Focus on promote flow. Soft-affinity still in place, but during node failure proxy should attempt local standby promote before rejecting writes.

Marta Ruiz: Residency rule recap: tenant writes must stay inside declared region unless explicit export flag set. If promote cannot complete, proxy must return error, not write remotely.

Diego Santos: Technical note: implement a short-lived lease with epoch fencing. Promoter sends fence request to control-plane: bump epoch, get confirmation. If confirmation fails within 20s, reject.

Ben Carter: Agree on epoch fencing. Also add header X-Effective-Region and X-Promote-Epoch to responses for validation and auditing.

Asha Rao: Test plan: flip feature-flag to allow local-standby-to-write, replay 2m of traffic for 30 tenants, assert: 1) zero non-EU write traces, 2) promote latency P95 < 800ms, 3) KV cache miss impact measured.

Marta Ruiz: Ops: avoid real VPC peering changes. We'll simulate promote with FF and mark writes with audit tag promote=true. ops-bot will create an incident if any non-EU write detected.

ops-bot: Simulation flag enabled for tenants: tenant-101..130. Alerting configured for non-EU write events.

Diego Santos: I'll add lease API: POST /v1/leases/{tenant} {epoch} -> 200 or 409. Proxy will require confirmed epoch to allow write path.

Ben Carter: Also gate metrics: promote_attempts, promote_success, promote_failures, blocked_writes. Dashboard widget pls.

Asha Rao: Owners: Diego = lease code + unit tests, Ben = telemetry + dashboard, Marta = runbook + ops config. Sync Friday to finalize. If no objections I'll schedule. :white_check_mark:
