title: #architecture: 1738765432
preview:
Priya Natarajan: quick RFC follow-up — thinking about making rollout metadata intentionally minimal on the wire and doing client-delta probing from the console. TL;DR: small pings for compatibility, server-side enrichment for ops. tradeoffs?
Ben Cho: +1 to minimalism, but worried about post-rollback debugging. we should keep at least: model, variant, pin_reason, expires_at/soft_ttl. otherwise ops blind.
Mateo Alvarez: +1. also how do we distinguish deprecation vs soft pin? suggest a compact enum like retire_hint: 'none'|'warn'|'soft_retire'|'hard_retire' so clients can surface warnings.
Priya Natarajan: one option. my proposal: wire payload stays tiny, something like:
```json
{"model":"redwood/llama-70b","variant":"q4o","pin_mode":"soft","expires_at":"2026-04-01T12:00:00Z"}
```
control plane enriches with deprecation hints, computed priority, origin, etc., only when a client opts in or for console UI.
Lina (eng-bot): deploy-bot: rollout-schema-v2 pushed to staging, schema validation tests: 45/50 passed, failing cases are optional fields parsing.
Ben Cho: practical concern — older SDKs will ignore expires_at. we need server-side grace/fallback window (eg: 72h default) + tenant override. otherwise pins could silently linger.
Mateo Alvarez: agreed. also when overlapping pins occur we need deterministic resolver rules (priority > created_ts > origin). should we encode 'priority' on-wire or keep it server-computed?
Priya Natarajan: prefer server-computed priority to keep wire small. but we can include a small optional debug view: origin and computed_priority when clients opt-in to richer payloads. that keeps default clients lean.
Ben Cho: sounds good. propose two small optional fields: "origin" (string) and "computed_priority" (int). optional, so normal clients ignore them. keeps debugging feasible.
Mateo Alvarez: telemetry-wise we should attach a tiny "delta_probe" sample: {last_seen_version, last_probe_ts, sdk_version}. very small, helps migration metrics.
Priya Natarajan: deprecation semantics need to be explicit in ops playbook: warn -> console alert only; soft_retire -> attempt autoreplace with compatible variant after N failures; hard_retire -> block new pins and trigger pager. include tenant/SLA exemptions for autoreplace.
Ben Cho: add constraint: autoreplace must respect tenant pin overrides and SLA tier — premium tenants opt-out of auto-migrations.
Mateo Alvarez: question for private installs: if control plane enriches metadata, should we sign that enrichment so on-host components can trust it?
Priya Natarajan: yes. for Private we should sign control-plane-issued enrichment payloads. hosted infra can rely on internal auth, but signature optional field for private installs.
Ben Cho: action items: (1) draft minimal wire schema doc + example payloads, (2) define server enrichment fields + signing rules, (3) implement grace window/fallback behavior, (4) add delta_probe telemetry proto, (5) update SDKs to parse optional debug fields.
Priya Natarajan: I'll draft the PR for the wire schema + example payloads and ping @Mateo Alvarez and @Ben Cho for review. thanks all.
text:
Priya Natarajan: quick RFC follow-up — thinking about making rollout metadata intentionally minimal on the wire and doing client-delta probing from the console. TL;DR: small pings for compatibility, server-side enrichment for ops. tradeoffs?
Ben Cho: +1 to minimalism, but worried about post-rollback debugging. we should keep at least: model, variant, pin_reason, expires_at/soft_ttl. otherwise ops blind.
Mateo Alvarez: +1. also how do we distinguish deprecation vs soft pin? suggest a compact enum like retire_hint: 'none'|'warn'|'soft_retire'|'hard_retire' so clients can surface warnings.
Priya Natarajan: one option. my proposal: wire payload stays tiny, something like:
```json
{"model":"redwood/llama-70b","variant":"q4o","pin_mode":"soft","expires_at":"2026-04-01T12:00:00Z"}
```
control plane enriches with deprecation hints, computed priority, origin, etc., only when a client opts in or for console UI.
Lina (eng-bot): deploy-bot: rollout-schema-v2 pushed to staging, schema validation tests: 45/50 passed, failing cases are optional fields parsing.
Ben Cho: practical concern — older SDKs will ignore expires_at. we need server-side grace/fallback window (eg: 72h default) + tenant override. otherwise pins could silently linger.
Mateo Alvarez: agreed. also when overlapping pins occur we need deterministic resolver rules (priority > created_ts > origin). should we encode 'priority' on-wire or keep it server-computed?
Priya Natarajan: prefer server-computed priority to keep wire small. but we can include a small optional debug view: origin and computed_priority when clients opt-in to richer payloads. that keeps default clients lean.
Ben Cho: sounds good. propose two small optional fields: "origin" (string) and "computed_priority" (int). optional, so normal clients ignore them. keeps debugging feasible.
Mateo Alvarez: telemetry-wise we should attach a tiny "delta_probe" sample: {last_seen_version, last_probe_ts, sdk_version}. very small, helps migration metrics.
Priya Natarajan: deprecation semantics need to be explicit in ops playbook: warn -> console alert only; soft_retire -> attempt autoreplace with compatible variant after N failures; hard_retire -> block new pins and trigger pager. include tenant/SLA exemptions for autoreplace.
Ben Cho: add constraint: autoreplace must respect tenant pin overrides and SLA tier — premium tenants opt-out of auto-migrations.
Mateo Alvarez: question for private installs: if control plane enriches metadata, should we sign that enrichment so on-host components can trust it?
Priya Natarajan: yes. for Private we should sign control-plane-issued enrichment payloads. hosted infra can rely on internal auth, but signature optional field for private installs.
Ben Cho: action items: (1) draft minimal wire schema doc + example payloads, (2) define server enrichment fields + signing rules, (3) implement grace window/fallback behavior, (4) add delta_probe telemetry proto, (5) update SDKs to parse optional debug fields.
Priya Natarajan: I'll draft the PR for the wire schema + example payloads and ping @Mateo Alvarez and @Ben Cho for review. thanks all.

title: #architecture: 1738703321
preview:
Talia Benson: Hey folks — need to schedule a design review for the delivery semantics ADR (audit export). This is the one covering at-least-once, idempotency, checkpointing, replay/backfill. Can we get 45m this week? Target attendees: @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei @Sean Gallagher @Rafael Mendes @Logan Wright @Jordan Lee. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency (draft)

Ethan Park: +1. We should lock this before we finalize the control plane config API behavior. I can do Thu 11-12 PT or Fri 2-3 PT.

Marcus Lin: I’m in Thu 11 PT. Main question from Sec side: are we *explicitly* saying at-least-once and pushing dedup downstream? We need crisp language for customers + SOC2 evidence.

Kevin Osei: Same. Also need to be super clear on retention vs export. “Guaranteed delivery” gets interpreted as “exactly-once” and “no gaps”, which we can’t promise in outages w/ throttling.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV: exactly-once is basically a trap here unless we’re willing to build transactional sinks + state per destination at scale. Prefer at-least-once with bounded replay + tooling. Also need safety valves when downstream is hard-down (auto-pause, max lag).

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If we do exactly-once semantics at the exporter level, we’ll be fighting duplicates *and* missing events forever. At-least-once + stable `event_id` + clear guidance that SIEM should dedup is the sane path.

Logan Wright: Observability angle: at-least-once is easier to reason about in metrics. We can expose lag, retry counts, DLQ rates. Exactly-once claims will generate tickets when customers see duplicates in Splunk.

Jordan Lee: Question: how do we define idempotency key? `event_id` is per event (globally unique) — but exporters will batch. Do we ever need a `delivery_id` per destination attempt?

Benji Okafor: Draft ADR says: primary dedup key is `event_id` (uuid v7-ish) emitted at audit event creation time. Exporter retries can send duplicates; downstream should dedup on `event_id`. For S3, we’re writing files (partitioned) so dedup is more “consumer dedup” unless they ingest into something.

Marcus Lin: I’m ok with `event_id` but we must ensure it’s stable + non-PII and exists for *all* events. Also: how do we handle events emitted pre-v2 schema? Are we going to backfill IDs or just say beta is v2 only?

Ethan Park: Beta will require audit schema v2 (ENG-5127). We can do a compatibility mapping but exporter should only export events that have `event_id`. Otherwise we’ll create non-deduppable events.

Kevin Osei: That’s fine but we need to be explicit in beta docs: “only v2 events exported; older events not backfilled.” Also define backfill window clearly.

Sean Gallagher: Another angle: we should avoid “exactly once” anywhere in docs/UI. It’s not even true for Splunk HEC unless we implement an ack + checkpoint at event granularity and handle every edge case.

Jordan Lee: What if we do: exporter persists checkpoint as “max event_time seen” and “last exported event_id”? That still has ordering assumptions.

Benji Okafor: Current plan is checkpoint by monotonically increasing cursor from the audit store (think `event_seq` / `insert_id`), not `event_time`. Time is not safe for cursoring. ADR mentions “cursor is storage-assigned, strictly increasing per tenant/workspace”.

Rafael Mendes: Yes please. Using timestamps for cursoring will cause gaps during clock skew / delayed ingestion.

Logan Wright: For Splunk: HEC returns 200 even if events are partially accepted? (depends). We should clarify what we consider “delivered”. If we treat HTTP 200 as success for the batch, duplicates will be possible on retry but acceptable.

Marcus Lin: That’s the customer confusion point. We need the language: “at-least-once to destination endpoint; customer should deduplicate using `event_id`.” Plus: “export can be paused during prolonged downstream outage; audit log in Redwood remains source of truth.”

Kevin Osei: +1. Also need explicit mention of DLQ and operator-assisted replay: “failed batches may be queued for replay; replay can result in duplicates.”

Connor O'Brien: On rollout mechanics: we also need a decision on what happens if a config changes while exporter is mid-stream. Do we reset checkpoint? pause? This impacts “duplicates vs missing”.

Ethan Park: Draft behavior: config changes do NOT reset checkpoint by default. If filters/destination change, we continue from last cursor. If customer wants backfill, they set `backfill_window` (bounded). That might re-send older events = duplicates. UI must warn.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max backfill window in beta (e.g., 7d) + rate limit replays. Otherwise someone will set 90d and nuke the exporter.

Talia Benson: Ok sounds like the meeting needs to cover:
1) Exactly-once vs at-least-once wording (customer-facing)
2) Cursor/checkpoint model (seq vs time)
3) Replay/backfill semantics + bounds
4) Destination-specific ack semantics (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
5) Safety valves (auto-pause / max lag)

Talia Benson: I’ll schedule Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. If you can’t make it, drop comments in ADR.

Google Calendar: Event created: “ADR Review: Audit Export Delivery Semantics + Idempotency” Thu Feb 6, 11:00–11:45am PT. Guests: Talia Benson, Ethan Park, Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei, Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes, Logan Wright, Jordan Lee, Benji Okafor, Connor O'Brien. Location: https://zoom.us/j/987654321

Marcus Lin: Thanks. I’ll add comments tonight on “what we can/can’t claim” for compliance.

Kevin Osei: I’ll also propose FAQ language: “at-least-once, may contain duplicates; use `event_id` for dedup.”

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review the failure modes section and ensure we’re not implicitly promising no duplicates.

Logan Wright: I’ll come with metrics/alerts we need to validate the semantics (lag, retry, DLQ depth).

Ethan Park: Great. Also Jordan/Benji can we make sure ADR calls out idempotency expectations per destination? Splunk/Datadog can dedup in pipeline; S3 is just object store.

Jordan Lee: yep, will add a note: S3 delivery is “at-least-once object writes” (possible duplicate objects) and consumer should handle. We should also use deterministic object keying where possible to reduce duplicates.

Benji Okafor: careful: deterministic keys can lead to overwrite = data loss if partial writes. Prefer unique keys + manifest? We can debate in the review.

Rafael Mendes: +1, overwrites are scary. Unique objects + checkpoint in our DB is safer; duplicates are OK.

Talia Benson: Perfect. See you Thu.

title: #architecture: 1737762209
preview:
Jada Williams: Kicking off ADR-034 review re: unit econ cost attribution model. Main open items: allocation basis, idle capacity treatment, and making sure this lines up w/ Finance methodology (Rishi/Laura). Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.net/display/ARCH/ADR-034-unit-economics-cost-attribution-model

Cole Summers: thx. tl;dr options we captured:
1) allocate compute spend by output tokens
2) allocate by total tokens (in+out)
3) allocate by "effective GPU time" (gpu_ms) from runtime
4) hybrid fallback (gpu_ms where available, else perf coefficient * tokens)

Rishi Malhotra: From FP&A side: we need a definition that reconciles to fleet spend inputs + rate card. Allocation basis matters less than (a) stability + (b) ability to explain deltas. But we cannot have a model that swings purely b/c prompt lengths changed.

Laura Bennett: +1. For exec KPI, we want something we can defend quarter over quarter. If Product runs an experiment that changes average prompt length, the metric should reflect true spend per *delivered output* but we need to be explicit about what drives it.

Logan Wright: One issue w/ pure output token allocation: prefill costs get shoved into output denom, so long-context prompts make $/1M output tokens look "worse" even if decode path is stable. That might be OK but needs interpretation guidance.

Nadia Rahman: If we go gpu_ms: instrumentation quality is the risk. We have good coverage on decode gpu_time in runtime, but prefill vs decode split is still inconsistent across some models + streaming paths (we're fixing). Also multi-tenant batching complicates request-level gpu attribution.

Mei Lin: Warehouse POV: easiest to implement + backfill is token-based. gpu_ms will require joining runtime metrics and the metering events w/ consistent request_id. doable, but will extend v1 timeline unless we accept partial coverage + "confidence" flag.

Dylan Brooks: I don't want v1 to depend on perfect gpu_ms coverage. But I also don't love "output tokens" as allocation basis for compute. The whole point is showing drivers like caching/batching; token-only hides those.

Cole Summers: Proposed in ADR draft: allocate *cost pool* by gpu_ms where available; compute gpu_ms per request from runtime (post-batching) so it naturally captures batching gains. For cases w/ missing gpu_ms, fallback to `model_profile_cost_per_token * total_tokens` (profiles maintained by Quentin + ML).

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: if we do hybrid, we need a clear data quality SLO: "% requests with gpu_ms present" + alert when it drops. Otherwise the metric will silently change its basis and Finance will (rightly) freak out.

Allison Grant: +1. also if fallback triggers, we should surface a banner in dashboard + tag the time buckets as degraded. We already do similar for trace sampling.

Rishi Malhotra: Hybrid is OK as long as:
- numerator ties to rate card + amortization rules
- we can quantify fallback usage (and ideally keep it low)
- changes in model_profile coefficients are versioned (so historical doesn’t get rewritten)

Jada Williams: On idle capacity: ADR currently says we include idle in blended metric (since exec cares about total economics) but we break out idle as separate component for drilldown. Any objections?

Laura Bennett: Include idle in blended, yes. BUT we need consistent treatment across Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private.
- Dedicated: if customer pays a commit, do we treat their reserved-but-idle as "their" cost or platform idle?
- Hosted: idle is platform overhead, allocate across requests.

Cole Summers: In draft we suggested:
- Hosted fleet idle: allocate across hosted requests proportional to gpu_ms (or fallback basis)
- Dedicated idle: allocate to that customer tier (Dedicated) but not to a specific customer_id in v1 (privacy + contract nuance)
- Private: out of scope for shared allocation; private deployments have their own cost pool by contract (but dashboard still reports their $/1M output tokens based on their metered usage + their specific rate card)

Logan Wright: re: dedicated: if we allocate dedicated idle to dedicated usage, $/1M output tokens will spike when a customer is quiet. That is "true" blended unit economics, but will surprise people. We should show two modes? "blended" vs "utilized".

Rishi Malhotra: Finance needs blended (includes idle) for KPI. But I agree we should provide a "utilization-adjusted" view for ops so Eng isn't chasing noise when a big dedicated customer pauses traffic. Can that be a secondary metric?

Jada Williams: ADR mentions a secondary metric: "marginal compute $/1M output tokens" (excludes idle + shared overhead). Not in v1 UI, but we can calculate in warehouse and hide behind internal toggle.

Dylan Brooks: That works. But make sure no one screenshots marginal and sends to execs :)

Allison Grant: We can gate it behind `unit_econ.show_marginal=true` + watermark "internal / non-FP&A" in UI.

Nadia Rahman: Another nuance: allocation basis for overhead (control plane, networking, oncall) shouldn't use gpu_ms. That stuff scales more with requests/throughput. ADR propose: overhead pool allocated by request_count weighted by output_tokens? Thoughts?

Laura Bennett: Keep overhead allocation simple + stable. For v1, I'm ok with allocating overhead proportional to output tokens (or total tokens) as long as you document it. Don’t overfit.

Rishi Malhotra: +1. For reconciliation, we’ll likely have:
- Compute pool (GPU): allocated by gpu_ms (hybrid fallback)
- Non-GPU variable costs (eg egress): allocate by bytes_out or output_tokens proxy
- Fixed overhead: allocate by output_tokens
Just version the methodology.

Mei Lin: If we do multiple pools, warehouse schema should store columns: `gpu_cost_usd`, `overhead_cost_usd`, `egress_cost_usd`, `idle_cost_usd` + total. Then dashboard can stack.

Sean Gallagher: Please also include `coverage_gpu_ms_pct` per hour/model/region/tier in the aggregate table so we can alert and show confidence.

Jada Williams: Sounds like consensus:
- Primary KPI remains blended $/1M output tokens (includes idle + overhead)
- Compute allocation by gpu_ms where available, fallback to versioned model_profile coefficients
- Idle included, but surfaced as component; dedicated idle attributed to dedicated tier (not per customer)
- Add coverage/confidence signals + SLOs
I’ll update ADR-034 with this and tag for signoff.

Laura Bennett: 👍 Works for me, pending seeing the exact reconciliation steps written out.

Rishi Malhotra: same. Please include a note on month-close: rate card updates are versioned and applied prospectively for reporting stability.

Cole Summers: ack. I’ll add a section: `rate_card_version` join key + backfill policy (no silent rewrites). Also will call out timezone/day-boundary alignment to Finance calendar.

Logan Wright: Can we add one explicit "Known limitation" in ADR: $/1M output tokens is sensitive to prompt length b/c prefill cost is in numerator but denom is output. That’s expected; dashboard should show avg input tokens + prefill/decode ratio.

Jada Williams: good call. Adding that + linking to runtime signals doc.

Sean Gallagher: When ADR lands, loop SRE for alert routing + oncall ownership. We want the dependency graph documented.

Jada Williams: will do. Next step: I’ll post revised ADR and request approvals from @Laura @Rishi @Dylan by EOW.

Sean Gallagher: Please also include `coverage_gpu_ms_pct` per hour/model/region/tier in the aggregate table so we can alert and show confidence.

Jada Williams: Sounds like consensus:
- Primary KPI remains blended $/1M output tokens (includes idle + overhead)
- Compute allocation by gpu_ms where available, fallback to versioned model_profile coefficients
- Idle included, but surfaced as component; dedicated idle attributed to dedicated tier (not per customer)
- Add coverage/confidence signals + SLOs
I’ll update ADR-034 with this and tag for signoff.

Laura Bennett: 👍 Works for me, pending seeing the exact reconciliation steps written out.

Rishi Malhotra: same. Please include a note on month-close: rate card updates are versioned and applied prospectively for reporting stability.

Cole Summers: ack. I’ll add a section: `rate_card_version` join key + backfill policy (no silent rewrites). Also will call out timezone/day-boundary alignment to Finance calendar.

Logan Wright: Can we add one explicit "Known limitation" in ADR: $/1M output tokens is sensitive to prompt length b/c prefill cost is in numerator but denom is output. That’s expected; dashboard should show avg input tokens + prefill/decode ratio.

Jada Williams: good call. Adding that + linking to runtime signals doc.

Sean Gallagher: When ADR lands, loop SRE for alert routing + oncall ownership. We want the dependency graph documented.

Jada Williams: will do. Next step: I’ll post revised ADR and request approvals from @Laura @Rishi @Dylan by EOW.

title: #architecture: 1737324412
preview:
Allison Grant: dropping the proposed audit event taxonomy naming conventions here for quick review. main goals: consistent, SIEM-friendly, low-cardinality. pls sanity check + call out footguns.

Allison Grant: current proposal (rough):
- `event_type`: lower_snake with dot segments: `<domain>.<entity>.<action>`
- action is verb in present tense (create/update/delete/rotate/enable/disable/export.configure/etc)
- no IDs in `event_type` ever
- avoid logging raw diffs; include controlled summaries

Jordan Lee: +1 on dot segments. just want to make sure we don’t end up with 12 variants of “update”. can we define a small verb set and stick to it?

Allison Grant: yep, verb set is intended to be enumerated. draft list is like: create|update|delete|enable|disable|rotate|revoke|login|logout|impersonate|configure|export|install|upgrade|rollback

Kevin Osei: from SIEM side, dot segments are fine. Splunk folks usually build `event_type="rbac.role.update"` queries. pls don’t use camelCase.

Logan Wright: main concern: people will want to stuff “details” into event_type (e.g. `policy.fallback.update.added_region_us-east-1`). should be hard no.

Allison Grant: hard no :slightly_smiling_face: details go into structured fields.

Ethan Park: question: do we want `rbac.role.update` or `rbac.role.updated`? i prefer present tense since it matches “action” field semantics.

Jordan Lee: present tense reads better in filters IMO. it’s an event record but the type is “what happened”. either way, pick one.

Marcus Lin: choose one and enforce. don’t bike shed for weeks :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: ok, present tense it is.

Nadia Rahman: also pls avoid `event_type` prefixes like `audit.` or `redwood.`. redundant and wastes bytes. if we need namespace, do it in `producer.service`.

Allison Grant: agreed. taxonomies should be deployment-agnostic. we have `deployment_mode` / `environment` fields separately.

Paula Mendes: can we talk cardinality? biggest offenders we’ve seen:
- `request_path`
- raw `user_agent`
- “diff” field with full JSON
- free-form `reason` / “note”

Logan Wright: +1. for coverage dashboards, if people put request_path we get insane dimension explosion.

Kevin Osei: on `user_agent`: some customers like it for investigations, but raw UA is too high cardinality. recommend normalized fields?

Allison Grant: proposed:
- keep `source.user_agent` optional but *normalized* (e.g., `"Chrome"`, `"curl"`, `"redwood-sdk"`), not the full string
- keep raw UA only in debug logs, not audit logs

Ethan Park: what about `source.ip`? that’s technically high cardinality but also necessary.

Allison Grant: `source.ip` is allowed (investigation-critical). we’ll keep it but treat it as “approved high-cardinality” and not index it in every internal metric.

Logan Wright: +1. in telemetry we can hash it or keep it out of labels. audit log itself can contain it.

Jordan Lee: for policy changes, people will want to include full before/after. can we define `change_summary` as a bounded structure?

Allison Grant: yep. suggestion:
- `change.action`: `"set"|"add"|"remove"` (enum)
- `change.fields`: allow-list of field names that changed (strings from enum)
- `change.before` / `change.after`: strings, capped length, must not contain secrets
- optional `change.diff_hash`: sha256 of canonicalized JSON if you really need integrity

Kevin Osei: love `diff_hash`. helps when exporter retries cause dupes and customer wants de-dupe / correlation.

Marcus Lin: careful: `before/after` values can leak PII if it’s an email, etc. make sure we have a classification table + redaction guidance.

Allison Grant: totally. we’re mapping every field to classification. and `before/after` is only for config-ish enums, not for user-provided text.

Nadia Rahman: concrete example might help service teams.

Allison Grant: example draft:
```
event_type=rbac.role.update
actor.type=user
actor.id=usr_...
actor.email_hash=sha256(...)
resource.type=rbac_role
resource.id=role_...
change.fields=["permissions","name"]
change.before="permissions:viewer"
change.after="permissions:admin"
```

Ethan Park: nit: `resource.type=rbac_role` vs `rbac.role`? want consistent shape.

Jordan Lee: i’d vote `resource.type` as enum-ish tokens like `rbac_role`, `api_key`, `routing_policy`. then `event_type` stays dotted.

Allison Grant: yep, that’s where we landed in the schema draft. `resource.type` is an enum registry.

Logan Wright: pls also ban `resource.name` unless it’s constrained. names are often user-provided.

Allison Grant: we can allow `resource.display_name` only if it’s a system-generated slug and capped (e.g. max 64 chars) + character allow-list.

Kevin Osei: for exports, customers sometimes want “project name”. if we can’t include it, ok, but at least include stable IDs so they can join on their side.

Allison Grant: yep: IDs always, names optional w/ constraints.

Paula Mendes: also: please don’t log full prompt text / model output in audit logs (i know it sounds obvious but we’ve seen it in the wild).

Marcus Lin: +1000. audit logs are not app logs.

Jordan Lee: for routing policy events, can we standardize naming like:
- `policy.routing.update`
- `policy.fallback.update`
- `policy.allowlist.update`
vs going deeper like `routing.policy.update`

Allison Grant: preference is domain-first by ownership surface:
- `routing_policy.update`
- `routing_policy.fallback.update`
BUT i’m worried that mixing underscores + dots might be confusing.

Logan Wright: keep it consistent: dots separate “levels”, underscores inside tokens. so `policy_engine.routing_policy.update` is too long. maybe `policy.routing.update` with resource.type to disambiguate.

Kevin Osei: from SIEM, shorter `event_type` is better. `policy.routing.update` is fine if resource.type says `routing_policy`.

Ethan Park: +1. event_type shouldn’t encode service internals. `policy.routing.update` is clear.

Allison Grant: ok, proposal update:
- use high-level domains: `rbac.*`, `credential.*`, `policy.*`, `export.*`, `retention.*`, `private_install.*`
- keep resource.type as the specific thing (`api_key`, `routing_policy`, `siem_destination`, `retention_policy`)

Jordan Lee: works. for private installer, do we really want `private_install.install`? that reads weird.

Allison Grant: maybe `private.install` / `private.upgrade` / `private.rollback`? consistent with domain = private.

Marcus Lin: ok with `private.*` as long as it’s explicitly for the Private product/installer actions.

Nadia Rahman: last request: can we document “do not include request_path” in schema examples + lints? people will do it by habit.

Allison Grant: yes. we’ll add lint rules:
- reject `request_path`
- reject any `*.raw` fields in audit events
- enforce length caps on free-text fields
- enforce `event_type` registry (no ad hoc strings)

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: if we do that + a small approved list of high-card fields (source.ip, trace_id, request_id), dashboards will stay sane.

Kevin Osei: pls include guidance on `user_agent` normalization in exporter docs too (customers ask).

Allison Grant: noted. i’ll update the draft and link in this thread once pushed. thanks all.

Marcus Lin: thanks. let’s lock naming this week so instrumentation doesn’t churn.

title: #architecture: 1737052880
preview:
Ethan Park: Kicking off the ADR discussion for optimization config versioning. Core question: do we make configs mutable with an “active” pointer, or immutable versions with promotion/rollback semantics on top? Want to land a decision this week so ENG-4821/4825 can stop thrashing.

Jordan Lee: +1. Also need to be crisp about what “promotion” means. Is it a state on the config object, or a separate record of “this scope is currently pinned to version X”? Those are very different for audit/RBAC.

Benji Okafor: Option A (mutable config): single config row per scope (tenant/model/region), updates in place. Rollback = write old values back. Fast/simple, but diff history becomes “best effort” unless we snapshot. Also concurrency gets gross.

Michael Nguyen: The “mutable” approach means we basically reinvent versioning via audit table anyway. We’ll need immutable snapshots for diff view + rollback reliability. Otherwise you can’t guarantee rollback is byte-for-byte to a known-good.

Noah Patel: From runtime side, please don’t do in-place mutation. Mixed worker states become harder to reason about. If each apply references a `config_version_id`, the applier can be idempotent (`already_applied(version_id) => noop`). With in-place mutation, you need content hashing + you still have races if the same ID changes out from under you.

Chloe Martin: Console angle: users expect to see a timeline: “v12 created by Benji, canary applied to us-east 10%, promoted to 100%, rolled back to v11”. That’s easiest when versions are immutable. “Promoted” feels like an event, not a property.

Ethan Park: So likely Option B: immutable `OptimizationConfigVersion` objects. But then how do we represent what’s “active”? A pointer per scope?

Jordan Lee: I’m leaning toward a separate assignment object. Like `OptimizationConfigBinding` (name TBD):
- scope keys: tenant_id, model, region (or allow wildcards later)
- env: hosted/dedicated/private? (or “deployment_id”)
- active_version_id
- updated_at, updated_by
And keep a history table of binding changes.

Benji Okafor: That’s basically: versions are content-addressed snapshots, bindings are the mutable part. Promotion = update binding in a “wider” scope or environment. Rollback = update binding back to a prior version. Makes sense.

Marcus Lin: Security hat: if “promotion” is a status field on the version (e.g., `status=PROD`), then you conflate “this version is prod *somewhere*” with access control. RBAC wants: who can change prod binding for tenant X vs who can create a version draft. Separate binding gives us clean permission boundaries.

Connor O'Brien: Release/process angle: if you put “prod” on the version, you’ll get weirdness in multi-region rollouts. One region could be on v12 while another is on v11, but version says PROD… which is it? With bindings, “prod-ue1 binding points to v12” is unambiguous.

Ethan Park: Counterpoint: binding tables can explode (tenant*model*region). We’ll need reasonable defaults/inheritance.

Jordan Lee: We can do a hierarchy later (global -> tenant -> tenant+model -> tenant+model+region). But even with just “exact scope only” v1, bindings still exist. The ADR can state we’re not solving inheritance initially.

Michael Nguyen: Storage-wise, bindings won’t be that bad if we’re only storing for opted-in scopes. Also we can compress via “scope string” like `tenant:abc/model:llama3-70b/region:us-east-1` and index it, but that’s implementation.

Noah Patel: Another runtime reason: on startup we can ask control plane “what binding applies to me?” and get a single `active_version_id`. Workers cache by version ID. If bindings mutate, workers still just see “active_version_id changed from 11 -> 12” and do a safe transaction.

Chloe Martin: UI question: do we show “promotion” as a lifecycle state on the version (Draft/Validated/Active/etc) or purely as events? Users like statuses.

Benji Okafor: We can have version metadata like `created`, `validated`, `validation_errors` but not “active”. “Active” depends on binding/scope.

Jordan Lee: +1. Make “validated” a property of version (result of semantic validation), but “applied/promoted/rolled back” are binding events.

Marcus Lin: Also audit: event taxonomy becomes cleaner:
- optimization_config_version.created
- optimization_config_version.validated
- optimization_config_binding.updated (apply/promote/rollback can just be reason codes)
Then SIEM queries are consistent.

Ethan Park: Ok, but product/ops wants explicit “promote” and “rollback” verbs. Can we preserve those semantics in the API even if under the hood it’s “update binding”?

Benji Okafor: Yep. API can expose endpoints:
- POST /versions (create)
- POST /versions/{id}/validate
- POST /bindings/{scope}/apply {version_id, rollout_policy}
- POST /bindings/{scope}/rollback {to_version_id}
Internally both apply/rollback just set `target_version_id` and create an event record.

Connor O'Brien: Please keep rollback as a first-class endpoint for tooling + incident bot. Even if it maps to “binding update,” the semantics differ (e.g., requires confirmation + emits rollback audit event + maybe enforces cooldown).

Noah Patel: One more thing: should rollback create a *new* version that is identical to old version, or re-point to the old version?

Michael Nguyen: Re-point. Creating a new identical version breaks “known-good” identity. Also diff/history becomes confusing (“why does v13 equal v11?”).

Jordan Lee: Agree. If someone needs to make a small edit, they create v14. Rollback should be a binding change referencing existing version.

Chloe Martin: UX: timeline entry “Rolled back from v12 -> v11” is very legible. If we create v13 identical, it looks like we shipped a new config rather than reverting.

Marcus Lin: Audit requirements also favor re-pointing. Rollback event should include `from_version_id` and `to_version_id`, plus `reason` and `incident_id` optionally.

Ethan Park: What about the argument that “rollback is a change” so you want an immutable record of *the action*? With re-pointing, the immutable record is the audit event + binding history row.

Jordan Lee: Exactly. Binding history is append-only (or at least we treat it as immutable) so we have the action log.

Benji Okafor: Implementation detail: we should make binding updates conditional (compare-and-swap) to avoid the apply/rollback race. Like `UPDATE bindings SET active_version_id=? WHERE scope=? AND active_version_id=?` and fail if someone else changed it.

Noah Patel: +1000. Runtime applier will also do CAS on “current applied version” locally. But control plane must guard too.

Connor O'Brien: Can we capture in ADR that “promotion” is not a version state, it’s a binding update potentially across a broader scope? This will matter when we do staged rollouts.

Ethan Park: Yes. Drafting ADR language:
1) Versions are immutable snapshots with metadata + validation results.
2) Active config per scope is determined by a separate binding (mutable) with full history.
3) Promotion/rollback are binding changes with explicit audit events.
4) Runtime consumes version IDs for idempotency.

Chloe Martin: Add: Console will label “Active in X scopes” by querying bindings; don’t store it on the version.

Marcus Lin: Please explicitly call out RBAC split:
- create version: optimize_config.version.create
- validate: optimize_config.version.validate
- update binding (apply/promote/rollback): optimize_config.binding.update with reason codes; prod requires stronger permission.

Jordan Lee: Also call out scoping semantics v1: no inheritance, no wildcard. Otherwise we’ll bikeshed another week.

Benji Okafor: Works for me. I’ll add a note that later we can support “environment aliases” (e.g., stable/latest) as bindings, but those are still bindings.

Noah Patel: One last runtime note: if bindings are the mutable pointer, please ensure we can fetch “current binding” and “version payload” separately with ETags. We want low-latency polling and avoid pulling full config each time.

Michael Nguyen: Yep, versions table can be cached aggressively; binding fetch is small. Also, binding history should be append-only to support the diff view and “who changed what.”

Ethan Park: Cool. I’ll update ADR draft with this decision and circulate for :eyes: today. If no strong objections by EOD tomorrow, we mark immutable+binding as chosen.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: please tag me when the ADR is ready; release checklist depends on these semantics.

Chloe Martin: :thumbsup: will align with Kenji on wording for “apply vs promote” in UI once ADR is locked.

Marcus Lin: :white_check_mark: security approves this direction. Just make sure rollback emits a distinct event name (not just “binding updated”) so customer audit queries are sane.

Jordan Lee: +1. We can keep “binding.updated” as the base event but also emit “binding.rolled_back” (or include action=ROLLBACK). Let’s make it explicit.

Ethan Park: Got it. Action item: I’ll propose explicit action enum + include `request_id` + `actor` in event payload. End thread.

Benji Okafor: We can have version metadata like `created`, `validated`, `validation_errors` but not “active”. “Active” depends on binding/scope.

Jordan Lee: +1. Make “validated” a property of version (result of semantic validation), but “applied/promoted/rolled back” are binding events.

Marcus Lin: Also audit: event taxonomy becomes cleaner:
- optimization_config_version.created
- optimization_config_version.validated
- optimization_config_binding.updated (apply/promote/rollback can just be reason codes)
Then SIEM queries are consistent.

Ethan Park: Ok, but product/ops wants explicit “promote” and “rollback” verbs. Can we preserve those semantics in the API even if under the hood it’s “update binding”?

Benji Okafor: Yep. API can expose endpoints:
- POST /versions (create)
- POST /versions/{id}/validate
- POST /bindings/{scope}/apply {version_id, rollout_policy}
- POST /bindings/{scope}/rollback {to_version_id}
Internally both apply/rollback just set `target_version_id` and create an event record.

Connor O'Brien: Please keep rollback as a first-class endpoint for tooling + incident bot. Even if it maps to “binding update,” the semantics differ (e.g., requires confirmation + emits rollback audit event + maybe enforces cooldown).

Noah Patel: One more thing: should rollback create a *new* version that is identical to old version, or re-point to the old version?

Michael Nguyen: Re-point. Creating a new identical version breaks “known-good” identity. Also diff/history becomes confusing (“why does v13 equal v11?”).

Jordan Lee: Agree. If someone needs to make a small edit, they create v14. Rollback should be a binding change referencing existing version.

Chloe Martin: UX: timeline entry “Rolled back from v12 -> v11” is very legible. If we create v13 identical, it looks like we shipped a new config rather than reverting.

Marcus Lin: Audit requirements also favor re-pointing. Rollback event should include `from_version_id` and `to_version_id`, plus `reason` and `incident_id` optionally.

Ethan Park: What about the argument that “rollback is a change” so you want an immutable record of *the action*? With re-pointing, the immutable record is the audit event + binding history row.

Jordan Lee: Exactly. Binding history is append-only (or at least we treat it as immutable) so we have the action log.

Benji Okafor: Implementation detail: we should make binding updates conditional (compare-and-swap) to avoid the apply/rollback race. Like `UPDATE bindings SET active_version_id=? WHERE scope=? AND active_version_id=?` and fail if someone else changed it.

Noah Patel: +1000. Runtime applier will also do CAS on “current applied version” locally. But control plane must guard too.

Connor O'Brien: Can we capture in ADR that “promotion” is not a version state, it’s a binding update potentially across a broader scope? This will matter when we do staged rollouts.

Ethan Park: Yes. Drafting ADR language:
1) Versions are immutable snapshots with metadata + validation results.
2) Active config per scope is determined by a separate binding (mutable) with full history.
3) Promotion/rollback are binding changes with explicit audit events.
4) Runtime consumes version IDs for idempotency.

Chloe Martin: Add: Console will label “Active in X scopes” by querying bindings; don’t store it on the version.

Marcus Lin: Please explicitly call out RBAC split:
- create version: optimize_config.version.create
- validate: optimize_config.version.validate
- update binding (apply/promote/rollback): optimize_config.binding.update with reason codes; prod requires stronger permission.

Jordan Lee: Also call out scoping semantics v1: no inheritance, no wildcard. Otherwise we’ll bikeshed another week.

Benji Okafor: Works for me. I’ll add a note that later we can support “environment aliases” (e.g., stable/latest) as bindings, but those are still bindings.

Noah Patel: One last runtime note: if bindings are the mutable pointer, please ensure we can fetch “current binding” and “version payload” separately with ETags. We want low-latency polling and avoid pulling full config each time.

Michael Nguyen: Yep, versions table can be cached aggressively; binding fetch is small. Also, binding history should be append-only to support the diff view and “who changed what.”

Ethan Park: Cool. I’ll update ADR draft with this decision and circulate for :eyes: today. If no strong objections by EOD tomorrow, we mark immutable+binding as chosen.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: please tag me when the ADR is ready; release checklist depends on these semantics.

Chloe Martin: :thumbsup: will align with Kenji on wording for “apply vs promote” in UI once ADR is locked.

Marcus Lin: :white_check_mark: security approves this direction. Just make sure rollback emits a distinct event name (not just “binding updated”) so customer audit queries are sane.

Jordan Lee: +1. We can keep “binding.updated” as the base event but also emit “binding.rolled_back” (or include action=ROLLBACK). Let’s make it explicit.

Ethan Park: Got it. Action item: I’ll propose explicit action enum + include `request_id` + `actor` in event payload. End thread.
